(Option C). Signs of active infection are important to assess before surgery but are more related to postponing surgery rather than directly preventing aspiration during the procedure.

(Option D). Exudate in the tonsillar area indicates infection or inflammation but does not pose a direct aspiration risk compared to loose teeth. It is not the priority assessment for airway safety.

Educational Objective
Before tonsillectomy, assessing for loose teeth is essential to prevent aspiration during anesthesia. Preoperative airway safety checks are critical in pediatric surgical care.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Preoperative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Loose teeth
Throat redness
Signs of active infection
Exudate in the tonsillar area

questionList.questionText: An HIV-positive woman delivers an infant. The physician prescribes testing for the newborn, and the nurse prepares to:
questionList.explanationText:
Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) testing in newborns differs from adults because maternal HIV antibodies (IgG) cross the placenta and may remain in the infant’s circulation for up to 18 months. Therefore, standard antibody tests such as ELISA and Western blot are unreliable in neonates. Instead, virologic testing (e.g., HIV RNA PCR or DNA PCR) is used to detect the actual presence of the virus. Early diagnosis is crucial to initiate antiretroviral therapy and reduce morbidity and mortality.

(Correct Option A). Asking the laboratory to perform virologic testing is correct because it directly detects the presence of HIV in the infant’s blood. This method is the standard for neonatal HIV diagnosis, as it avoids false-positive results caused by transplacental maternal antibodies.

(Option B). Obtaining blood from the umbilical cord is not recommended because it may be contaminated with maternal blood, leading to inaccurate results. Peripheral blood samples are preferred for reliable testing.

(Option C). Western blot testing is inappropriate for newborns because it detects antibodies, which may reflect maternal antibodies rather than true infection in the infant.

(Option D). ELISA testing is also antibody-based and therefore unreliable in neonates due to the presence of maternal HIV antibodies, which can produce false-positive results.

Educational Objective
Newborns of HIV-positive mothers require virologic testing (PCR) rather than antibody-based tests. This ensures accurate early diagnosis and timely initiation of treatment.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: HIV in Newborns

answerChoiceList.choice: Ask the laboratory to perform virologic testing
Obtain blood from the umbilical cord to send to the laboratory
Perform a heelstick to obtain a specimen for a Western blot assay
Perform a fingerstick to obtain a specimen for an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA)

questionList.questionText: A nurse is assessing a child after tonsillectomy. Which finding is indicative of postoperative bleeding?
questionList.explanationText:
Postoperative bleeding after a tonsillectomy is a serious complication that requires immediate recognition. Because the surgical site is located in the throat, bleeding may not always be visible externally. Instead, children may exhibit subtle signs such as frequent swallowing, which occurs as blood trickles down the throat. Other signs can include restlessness, tachycardia, pallor, and vomiting of blood. Early identification is critical to prevent airway obstruction and hypovolemic shock.

(Correct Option B). Frequent swallowing is a key indicator of postoperative bleeding because the child may be swallowing blood from the surgical site. This is often one of the earliest and most reliable signs of internal bleeding in the throat following tonsillectomy.

(Option A). A slowed pulse rate is not expected in bleeding. Instead, bleeding typically causes tachycardia as the body attempts to compensate for blood loss.

(Option C). Complaints of throat pain are expected after tonsillectomy and are a normal postoperative finding. Pain alone does not indicate bleeding.

(Option D). An increase in blood pressure is not a typical early sign of bleeding. In significant blood loss, blood pressure may eventually decrease rather than increase.

Educational Objective
Frequent swallowing after tonsillectomy is an early sign of postoperative bleeding. Prompt recognition is essential to prevent airway compromise and serious complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Postoperative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Slowed pulse rate
Frequent swallowing
Complaints of throat pain
An increase in blood pressure

questionList.questionText: In which position should the nurse place the child who has just undergone tonsillectomy to facilitate drainage?
questionList.explanationText:
Tonsillectomy is a surgical procedure that places the child at risk for airway obstruction and postoperative bleeding. Immediately after surgery, it is essential to position the child in a way that promotes drainage of secretions and blood from the mouth and throat to prevent aspiration. The prone or side-lying position allows gravity to facilitate drainage and helps maintain a clear airway. Proper positioning is a critical nursing intervention to ensure respiratory safety and prevent complications.

(Correct Option A). The prone position is correct because it promotes effective drainage of secretions and blood from the oropharynx, reducing the risk of aspiration and airway obstruction. This position is especially important immediately after surgery when protective reflexes may still be diminished.

(Option B). The supine position is incorrect because it allows secretions to pool in the back of the throat, increasing the risk of aspiration and airway compromise.

(Option C). High Fowler position may be used later for comfort, but it is not the best immediate postoperative position for promoting drainage in a child who is still recovering from anesthesia.

(Option D). Semi-Fowler position provides some elevation but does not facilitate drainage as effectively as the prone or side-lying position, especially in the immediate postoperative period.

Educational Objective
After tonsillectomy, placing the child in a prone or side-lying position helps promote drainage and prevent aspiration. Proper positioning is essential for airway protection in the immediate postoperative period.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Postoperative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Prone
Supine
High Fowler
Semi-Fowler

questionList.questionText: Hemosiderosis develops in a child with beta-thalassemia as a result of long-term transfusion therapy. The child is being treated with deferoxamine (Desferal). The nurse assesses the effectiveness of this therapy by monitoring the child’s:
questionList.explanationText:
Beta-thalassemia is a genetic blood disorder characterized by defective hemoglobin synthesis, leading to chronic anemia that often requires repeated blood transfusions. Over time, these transfusions cause iron overload (hemosiderosis), where excess iron deposits in organs such as the liver, heart, and endocrine glands. Deferoxamine (Desferal) is an iron-chelating agent that binds excess iron and promotes its excretion. The effectiveness of this therapy is evaluated by monitoring serum iron levels, which reflect the body’s iron burden and response to treatment.

(Correct Option C). Monitoring the serum iron level is correct because deferoxamine works by reducing excess iron in the body. A decrease in serum iron indicates that the chelation therapy is effective in removing iron and preventing complications of iron overload such as organ damage.

(Option A). Lung sounds are not a direct indicator of iron overload or the effectiveness of deferoxamine therapy. Although respiratory status is important, it does not reflect iron levels in the body.

(Option B). Blood pressure monitoring is essential for overall assessment but does not provide information about iron overload or response to chelation therapy.

(Option D). Serum erythrocyte levels (RBC count) reflect anemia status but do not indicate iron overload or the effectiveness of iron-chelating treatment.

Educational Objective
In beta-thalassemia, repeated transfusions lead to iron overload. Deferoxamine therapy effectiveness is best assessed by monitoring serum iron levels to ensure proper removal of excess iron.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Thalassemia Management

answerChoiceList.choice: Lung sounds
Blood pressure
Serum iron level
Serum erythrocyte level

questionList.questionText: A nurse is performing an assessment of a child with nephrotic syndrome. Which manifestation would the nurse most likely note?
questionList.explanationText:
Nephrotic syndrome is a renal disorder characterized by increased glomerular permeability, leading to significant protein loss in urine (proteinuria). This protein loss results in hypoalbuminemia, which decreases plasma oncotic pressure and causes fluid to shift into the interstitial spaces, producing edema. One of the earliest and most noticeable signs in children is periorbital edema, especially in the morning. Other manifestations include weight gain due to fluid retention, fatigue, and ascites. Blood pressure is typically normal or slightly decreased rather than elevated.

(Correct Option 1). Periorbital edema is a classic and early sign of nephrotic syndrome in children. It occurs due to fluid accumulation from decreased oncotic pressure caused by hypoalbuminemia. The swelling is often most noticeable around the eyes and may progress to generalized edema.

(Option 2). Weight loss is incorrect because children with nephrotic syndrome usually experience weight gain due to fluid retention and edema rather than losing weight.

(Option 3). A temperature of 99.2°F is within the normal or low-grade range and is not a defining feature of nephrotic syndrome. Fever may indicate infection, but it is not a typical manifestation of the syndrome itself.

(Option 4). Elevated blood pressure is more commonly associated with nephritic syndrome rather than nephrotic syndrome. In nephrotic syndrome, blood pressure is usually normal or slightly decreased due to fluid shifts.

Educational Objective
Nephrotic syndrome is characterized by proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, and edema. Periorbital edema is an early and hallmark sign in children due to decreased plasma oncotic pressure and fluid shifting into tissues.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Nephrotic Syndrome

answerChoiceList.choice: Periorbital edema
Weight loss of 1.5 kg
Temperature of 99.2° F
Blood pressure of 128/86 mm Hg

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: The nurse should contact the healthcare provider with concerns about a prescription for valproic acid (Depakene) for an adolescent who has a history of:
questionList.explanationText:
Valproic acid (Depakene) is an anticonvulsant medication commonly used to treat seizure disorders, bipolar disorder, and migraine prophylaxis. However, it is known to cause hepatotoxicity, particularly in patients with pre-existing liver conditions. The drug is metabolized in the liver and can lead to elevated liver enzymes, liver failure, and fatal hepatotoxicity. Therefore, it is contraindicated in individuals with hepatic disease. Nurses must carefully assess patient history for liver disorders and monitor liver function tests (LFTs) before and during therapy to prevent serious complications.

(Correct Option 1). A history of hepatitis is a major contraindication for valproic acid because the medication can cause severe liver damage. Since the liver is already compromised, administering this drug can increase the risk of acute liver failure. Therefore, the nurse should immediately contact the healthcare provider.

(Option 2). Diabetes mellitus is not a contraindication for valproic acid. Although metabolic changes can occur, it does not pose the same level of risk as liver disease.

(Option 3). Migraine headaches is actually an indication for valproic acid use, as the medication is commonly prescribed for migraine prophylaxis. Therefore, this would not raise concern.

(Option 4). Tonic-clonic seizures are one of the primary indications for valproic acid therapy. The drug is effective in controlling these types of seizures and is appropriate for use in such patients.

Educational Objective
Valproic acid is contraindicated in patients with liver disease due to its risk of hepatotoxicity. Always assess liver history and monitor liver function tests before and during treatment.
questionList.subject: Pharmacology
questionList.title: Anticonvulsants

answerChoiceList.choice: Hepatitis
Diabetes mellitus
Migraine headaches
Tonic-clonic seizures

questionList.questionText: The nurse assesses a child with suspected meningitis for the presence of the Kernig sign. Which finding is the Kernig sign?
questionList.explanationText:
Meningitis is an inflammation of the meninges, the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord. This condition leads to meningeal irritation, which produces characteristic physical signs such as Kernig sign and Brudzinski sign. The Kernig sign is elicited by flexing the patient's hip and knee, followed by attempting to extend the knee. A positive sign occurs when there is pain or resistance in the hamstrings during knee extension, indicating irritation of the meninges. This response results from stretching of inflamed nerve roots. These clinical signs are important for early detection of meningitis, which can be life-threatening if not treated promptly.

(Correct Option 2). Pain with extension of the leg and knee correctly describes a positive Kernig sign. When the nurse attempts to extend the knee while the hip is flexed, the patient experiences pain due to meningeal irritation and stretching of inflamed nerve roots.

(Option 1). Calf pain on dorsiflexion of the foot describes Homans' sign, which is associated with deep vein thrombosis (DVT), not meningitis.

(Option 3). Flexion of the hips and knees when the head is flexed describes Brudzinski sign, another indicator of meningeal irritation but different from Kernig sign.

(Option 4). Calf pain when the calf muscle is squeezed against the tibia is also related to DVT assessment and is not associated with meningitis.

Educational Objective
Kernig sign is a clinical indicator of meningeal irritation, characterized by pain on extension of the knee when the hip is flexed. It helps in early recognition of meningitis.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Meningitis Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Calf pain on dorsiflexion of the foot
Pain with extension of the leg and knee
Flexion of the hips and knees when the head is flexed
Calf pain when the calf muscle is squeezed against the tibia

questionList.questionText: An immunocompromised child who never had chickenpox is exposed to a child with varicella. The nurse should tell the mother of the immunocompromised child that:
questionList.explanationText:
Varicella (chickenpox) is a highly contagious viral infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus (VZV). In immunocompromised children, the infection can become severe and lead to complications such as pneumonia, encephalitis, and disseminated disease. These children lack adequate immune defenses to control viral replication. Therefore, after exposure, immediate passive immunization with varicella-zoster immune globulin (VZIG) is recommended to provide temporary antibodies and reduce the severity of infection. Early intervention is critical because active infection can be life-threatening in this population.

(Correct Option 1). The child will receive varicella zoster immune globulin, which provides passive immunity to help prevent or reduce the severity of infection. This is the recommended intervention for high-risk individuals, including immunocompromised children who have been exposed to varicella.

(Option 2). It is incorrect to say there is no concern because varicella infection can be severe and life-threatening in immunocompromised children. Immediate preventive measures are necessary.

(Option 3). Hospitalization and respiratory isolation are not required unless the child becomes infected or develops severe symptoms. The priority is preventive immunoglobulin administration after exposure.

(Option 4). Monitoring for symptoms alone is insufficient because prophylactic treatment is required to reduce the risk and severity of infection in immunocompromised patients.

Educational Objective
Immunocompromised children exposed to varicella require varicella-zoster immune globulin for passive immunity to prevent severe infection. Early intervention is essential to reduce complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Varicella Management

answerChoiceList.choice: The child will receive varicella zoster immune globulin
There is no need to be concerned about the exposure to varicella
The child will be hospitalized and placed in respiratory isolation
The child should be monitored closely for early signs of chickenpox

questionList.questionText: A nurse is performing an assessment of a child admitted to the hospital with suspected rheumatic fever. About which recent occurrence should the nurse ask the parents as a means of eliciting data relevant to the cause of illness?
questionList.explanationText:
Rheumatic fever is an inflammatory autoimmune condition that develops as a complication of untreated or inadequately treated group A beta-hemolytic streptococcal (GAS) infection, most commonly streptococcal pharyngitis (strep throat). The body produces antibodies against the bacteria, but these antibodies mistakenly attack cardiac tissue, joints, skin, and the central nervous system, leading to manifestations such as carditis, polyarthritis, chorea, and skin lesions. Therefore, identifying a recent history of sore throat is critical, as it is the primary antecedent event that triggers rheumatic fever.

(Correct Option 1). A recent sore throat is highly significant because it indicates a possible streptococcal infection, which is the primary cause of rheumatic fever. The nurse should assess for untreated or recent strep throat to support the diagnosis.

(Option 2). A blunt chest injury is unrelated to rheumatic fever, as this condition is not caused by trauma but by an immune response to infection.

(Option 3). A swollen knee joint may be a symptom of rheumatic fever (polyarthritis), but it is not the underlying cause. The question asks about the causative factor, not manifestations.

(Option 4). Recent loss of appetite is a nonspecific symptom and does not directly relate to the cause of rheumatic fever. It may occur with many illnesses but is not diagnostic.

Educational Objective
Rheumatic fever is a complication of untreated streptococcal throat infection. A history of sore throat is a key clue in identifying the underlying cause of the disease.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Rheumatic Fever

answerChoiceList.choice: A sore throat
Blunt chest injury
A swollen knee joint
Recent loss of appetite

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides instructions on the administration of oral iron to the mother of a child with iron-deficiency anemia. The nurse determines that the mother understands the instructions if the mother states that she will administer the iron with:
questionList.explanationText:
Iron-deficiency anemia occurs when there is insufficient iron availability for hemoglobin production, leading to decreased oxygen-carrying capacity of red blood cells. Oral iron supplements are commonly prescribed, and their absorption is significantly influenced by dietary factors. Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) enhances iron absorption by converting iron into a more absorbable form in the gastrointestinal tract. Conversely, substances like milk, calcium, cereals, and formula can inhibit iron absorption by forming insoluble complexes. Therefore, proper administration techniques are essential to maximize therapeutic effectiveness.

(Correct Option 4). Orange juice is rich in vitamin C, which enhances the absorption of iron in the gastrointestinal tract. Administering iron with orange juice improves its bioavailability and ensures more effective treatment of anemia.

(Option 1). Milk contains calcium, which interferes with iron absorption by forming insoluble compounds. Therefore, iron should not be administered with milk.

(Option 2). Cereal, especially those high in fiber or phytates, can bind to iron and reduce its absorption. It is not recommended to give iron supplements with cereal.

(Option 3). Formula may also contain calcium and other components that inhibit iron absorption, making it a less effective medium for administering iron.

Educational Objective
Oral iron absorption is enhanced by vitamin C and inhibited by calcium-containing foods. Administer iron with orange juice to maximize absorption and therapeutic effectiveness.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Iron-Deficiency Anemia

answerChoiceList.choice: Milk
Cereal
Formula
Orange juice

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questionList.questionText: A nurse reviews the physician’s prescriptions for the care of a child who has just undergone tonsillectomy. Which prescription should the nurse question?
questionList.explanationText:
Tonsillectomy is a surgical procedure involving removal of the tonsils, and postoperative care focuses on preventing bleeding, airway obstruction, and infection. The surgical site is highly sensitive, and any unnecessary irritation can lead to hemorrhage, which is a major complication. Suctioning of the throat can disrupt the surgical site, dislodge clots, and increase the risk of bleeding. Therefore, suctioning is generally avoided unless absolutely necessary and performed cautiously. Proper care includes positioning the child on the side to prevent aspiration, using cold therapy (ice collar) to reduce swelling, and providing cool, clear fluids once the child is awake to maintain hydration without irritating the throat.

(Correct Option 1). Suction every 2 hours should be questioned because frequent suctioning can irritate the surgical site and increase the risk of postoperative bleeding. It is contraindicated unless there is airway obstruction and must be done very carefully if required.

(Option 2). Positioning the child on the side is appropriate because it helps prevent aspiration of blood and secretions and maintains airway patency.

(Option 3). Using an ice collar is beneficial as it helps reduce swelling and pain by providing local vasoconstriction.

(Option 4). Providing clear, cool liquids when the child is fully awake is appropriate to maintain hydration and avoid irritation of the surgical site.

Educational Objective
After tonsillectomy, avoid procedures like frequent suctioning that may cause bleeding. Focus on airway protection, pain relief, and gentle hydration to promote safe recovery.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Postoperative Care - Tonsillectomy

answerChoiceList.choice: Suction every 2 hours.
Position the child on his side.
Use an ice collar as needed for comfort.
Provide clear, cool liquids when the child is fully awake.

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse receives a telephone call from the admissions office and is told that a child with acute bacterial meningitis will be admitted to the pediatric unit. The nurse prepares for the child’s arrival and plans to implement:
questionList.explanationText:
Acute bacterial meningitis is a serious infection of the meninges caused by organisms such as Neisseria meningitidis or Streptococcus pneumoniae. These pathogens are transmitted through respiratory droplets expelled during coughing, sneezing, or close contact. Because of this mode of transmission, strict infection control precautions are required to prevent spread to healthcare workers and other patients. Droplet precautions include wearing a mask when within close proximity (usually within 3 feet), placing the patient in a private room if possible, and ensuring proper hand hygiene. These precautions are critical during the initial phase of illness, especially within the first 24 hours of antibiotic therapy.

(Correct Option 3). Droplet precautions are required because bacterial meningitis spreads through respiratory droplets. Implementing these precautions helps prevent transmission to healthcare providers and other patients.

(Option 1). Enteric precautions are used for infections spread via the fecal-oral route, such as gastrointestinal infections, and are not appropriate for meningitis.

(Option 2). Contact precautions are used for infections transmitted by direct or indirect contact, such as MRSA, but meningitis is primarily spread via droplets.

(Option 4). Neutropenic precautions are used to protect immunocompromised patients from infection, not to prevent transmission of infectious diseases like meningitis.

Educational Objective
Bacterial meningitis is transmitted via respiratory droplets, requiring droplet precautions to prevent spread. Early implementation of these precautions is essential for infection control.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Infection Control - Meningitis

answerChoiceList.choice: Enteric precautions
Contact precautions
Droplet precautions
Neutropenic precautions

questionList.questionText: What discharge instructions are important to provide the parents after their child undergoes cardiac catheterization? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Cardiac catheterization is an invasive diagnostic and therapeutic procedure used to evaluate and treat cardiac conditions. After the procedure, proper discharge instructions are essential to prevent complications such as bleeding, infection, and injury at the catheter insertion site. Parents should be instructed to monitor for signs of infection (fever, redness, swelling), avoid activities that could disrupt healing, and manage discomfort appropriately. Strenuous activities and contact sports should be restricted for a period to prevent injury to the insertion site. Additionally, mild discomfort is common and can be managed with analgesics like acetaminophen or ibuprofen.

(Correct Option 4). Contact sports should be avoided for 1 week to prevent trauma to the catheter insertion site, which could lead to bleeding or vessel damage. Activity restriction is essential for proper healing.

(Correct Option 5). Acetaminophen or ibuprofen may be given to relieve mild pain or discomfort after the procedure. These medications are safe and effective for managing post-procedural pain.

(Option 1). Fever is not normal after cardiac catheterization and may indicate infection. Parents should be instructed to report fever immediately.

(Option 2). Bleeding from the insertion site is not expected and may indicate a complication. Any bleeding should be reported promptly.

(Option 3). A tub bath is not recommended within 1 day because soaking the site can increase the risk of infection. Typically, showers are allowed after a specified period, but immersion should be avoided initially.

Educational Objective
After cardiac catheterization, parents should restrict strenuous activity, monitor for complications, and manage discomfort with appropriate analgesics. Avoid actions that increase risk of bleeding or infection.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Cardiac Catheterization Care

answerChoiceList.choice: A fever is normal after the procedure.
Some bleeding from the catheter insertion site is expected.
The child may play in a tub bath 1 day after the procedure.
Contact sports should be avoided for 1 week after the procedure.
Acetaminophen or ibuprofen may be given to ease pain or discomfort.

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText:
A physician prescribes the following interventions for a child with suspected meningitis. Prioritize these interventions by numbering them in the order in which they should be performed, with 1 as the first prescription to be carried out and 4 as the last.

1. Lumbar puncture
2. Respiratory isolation
3. Continued neurologic assessments every 2 hours
4. Administer antibiotics
questionList.explanationText:
Meningitis is a life-threatening infection of the central nervous system requiring rapid and prioritized interventions to prevent complications such as increased intracranial pressure, neurological damage, and death. The first priority is to prevent transmission by initiating respiratory (droplet) isolation. Next, a lumbar puncture is performed to obtain cerebrospinal fluid for diagnosis before antibiotic therapy when possible. After diagnostic specimens are collected, antibiotics should be administered promptly to treat the infection. Finally, ongoing neurologic assessments are essential for monitoring disease progression and response to treatment.

(Correct Option 2). The correct sequence is 2, 1, 4, 3. First, implement respiratory isolation to prevent spread. Second, perform a lumbar puncture to obtain diagnostic data. Third, administer antibiotics promptly after cultures are obtained. Lastly, continue neurologic assessments to monitor the child's condition.

(Option 1). Starting with lumbar puncture first ignores the priority of infection control and could expose others to infection.

(Option 3). Performing neurologic assessments early is important but does not take priority over infection control, diagnosis, and treatment.

(Option 4). Administering antibiotics before lumbar puncture may interfere with diagnostic results unless the child is unstable; thus, it is not the correct initial step in this sequence.

Educational Objective
In suspected meningitis, prioritize infection control first, followed by diagnostic testing, then prompt antibiotic therapy, and finally ongoing monitoring to ensure optimal outcomes.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Meningitis Management

questionList.questionText: A nurse is providing home care instructions to the mother of a child with sinusitis. Which statement by the mother indicates a need for further instruction?
questionList.explanationText:
Sinusitis is an inflammation of the paranasal sinuses, often caused by infection or allergic reactions. This condition leads to mucosal swelling, obstruction of sinus drainage, and accumulation of secretions, resulting in symptoms such as facial pain, nasal congestion, and fever. Effective home management focuses on promoting sinus drainage, hydration, and symptom relief. Warm, moist air (humidification) helps loosen secretions, whereas cool air is less effective for promoting drainage. Analgesics like acetaminophen relieve discomfort, and adequate fluid intake helps thin mucus secretions.

(Correct Option 3). The statement "Breathing cool, moist air will help drain his sinuses" indicates a need for further instruction because warm, moist air is more effective in promoting sinus drainage and relieving congestion. Cool air does not provide the same therapeutic benefit.

(Option 1). Tylenol (acetaminophen) is appropriate for relieving pain and fever associated with sinusitis and is a correct understanding.

(Option 2). Encouraging clear liquids is correct because hydration helps thin mucus secretions and promotes sinus drainage.

(Option 4). Applying warm, moist compresses over the sinuses helps relieve pain and pressure and promotes drainage, making it an appropriate measure.

Educational Objective
In sinusitis, warm, moist air and hydration promote sinus drainage. Cool air is less effective, and proper symptom management includes analgesics and warm compresses.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Sinusitis Care

answerChoiceList.choice: “Tylenol can help relieve the discomfort.”
“I need to encourage him to drink clear liquids.”
“Breathing cool, moist air will help drain his sinuses.”
“I can put warm, moist compresses over his sinuses to make him feel better.”

questionList.questionText: A nurse is providing information to the mother of a child with newly diagnosed celiac disease. What piece of information should the nurse include?
questionList.explanationText:
Celiac disease is a chronic autoimmune disorder triggered by ingestion of gluten, a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye. In affected individuals, gluten exposure leads to an immune-mediated inflammatory response that damages the intestinal villi, resulting in malabsorption of nutrients. This can cause symptoms such as diarrhea, weight loss, abdominal distention, and nutrient deficiencies. Additionally, stressors such as infection or surgery can precipitate a severe complication known as a celiac crisis, characterized by profuse diarrhea, dehydration, and electrolyte imbalance, making it a medical emergency.

(Correct Option 1). The statement that "An infection can precipitate a celiac crisis" is correct because physiologic stressors, including infections, can trigger an acute worsening of the disease. This may lead to severe malabsorption, dehydration, and metabolic disturbances, requiring prompt medical attention.

(Option 2). Celiac disease cannot be cured with medication. It is a lifelong condition managed with a strict gluten-free diet, not pharmacologic therapy.

(Option 3). Pasta is not appropriate because it typically contains wheat (gluten), which triggers the disease and causes intestinal damage.

(Option 4). Dietary modifications are not temporary; they must be maintained lifelong to prevent recurrence of symptoms and intestinal injury.

Educational Objective
Celiac disease is a lifelong autoimmune condition requiring a strict gluten-free diet. Infections and stressors can precipitate a celiac crisis, a serious complication requiring prompt recognition and management.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Celiac Disease

answerChoiceList.choice: An infection can precipitate a celiac crisis.
The disease can be cured with medication.
Pasta is an appropriate part of the child’s diet.
Temporary dietary modifications may be necessary to heal the gastrointestinal tract.

questionList.questionText: A nurse is admitting a child with respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection to the hospital. The nurse tells the parents that the best way to prevent the spread of the infection is:
questionList.explanationText:
Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a highly contagious virus that primarily affects the respiratory tract, especially in infants and young children. It spreads through direct contact with respiratory secretions or contaminated surfaces (fomites), rather than exclusively through airborne transmission. The virus can survive on surfaces for several hours, making hand contamination a major route of transmission. Therefore, meticulous hand hygiene is the most effective and critical intervention to prevent spread. Although contact precautions such as gowns and gloves may be used in healthcare settings, handwashing remains the single most important preventive measure to interrupt transmission.

(Correct Option 3). Washing the hands meticulously is the best method to prevent the spread of RSV because the virus is primarily transmitted through direct contact with contaminated hands and surfaces. Proper hand hygiene effectively removes the virus and significantly reduces transmission risk.

(Option 1). Restricting visitors may help reduce exposure but is not the most effective primary measure compared to strict hand hygiene.

(Option 2). Wearing goggles and a mask provides protection from droplets but does not address the most common mode of RSV transmission, which is contact spread.

(Option 4). Wearing goggles and a protective gown is part of contact precautions in healthcare settings, but handwashing is still the most essential and effective intervention.

Educational Objective
RSV spreads primarily through direct contact with respiratory secretions. Meticulous hand hygiene is the most effective way to prevent transmission and protect others.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: RSV Infection Control

answerChoiceList.choice: Restricting visitors
Wearing goggles and a mask
Washing the hands meticulously
Wearing goggles and a protective gown

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for an infant with hypospadias. The nurse makes a priority of assessing the infant’s:
questionList.explanationText:
Hypospadias is a congenital malformation in which the urethral opening is located on the underside of the penis rather than at the tip. This abnormal positioning can lead to difficulty with urinary flow, spraying of urine, and potential obstruction. Because the condition directly affects the urinary system, monitoring urinary elimination is essential to ensure that the infant is voiding adequately and that there are no complications such as urinary retention or obstruction. Early detection of impaired urinary output is critical to prevent kidney damage and infection, making it the highest priority in nursing assessment.

(Correct Option 2). Assessing urinary output is the priority because hypospadias directly affects the urethral opening and urinary flow. Monitoring output helps ensure that the infant is able to void effectively without obstruction, which is essential for preventing complications.

(Option 1). Monitoring blood pressure is important in general assessment but is not directly affected by hypospadias and is not the priority concern in this condition.

(Option 3). Level of consciousness is typically unrelated to hypospadias, as this condition does not affect the neurological system.

(Option 4). Gastrointestinal function is not impacted by hypospadias and therefore is not a priority assessment in this case.

Educational Objective
Hypospadias affects the urinary tract, making urinary output assessment the priority. Early identification of voiding problems helps prevent urinary complications and renal damage.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Hypospadias

answerChoiceList.choice: Blood pressure
Urinary output
Level of consciousness
Gastrointestinal function

questionList.questionText: A child’s mother tells the nurse that during a seizure the child has a blank expression and exhibits eyelid fluttering lasting just 5 to 10 seconds. The nurse determines that the child is experiencing:
questionList.explanationText:
Seizure disorders are characterized by abnormal electrical activity in the brain, resulting in different types of clinical manifestations depending on the area involved. Absence seizures (formerly called petit mal seizures) are a type of generalized seizure commonly seen in children and are characterized by brief lapses in consciousness without loss of postural control. These seizures typically last 5 to 10 seconds and present with a blank stare, unresponsiveness, and subtle motor signs such as eyelid fluttering or lip smacking. There is usually no postictal confusion, and the child quickly resumes normal activity. These features distinguish absence seizures from other seizure types.

(Correct Option 2). Absence seizures are characterized by a brief blank expression and eyelid fluttering lasting a few seconds. The child appears momentarily unaware and then resumes activity without confusion, which matches the description given.

(Option 1). Atonic seizures involve a sudden loss of muscle tone, causing the child to collapse or fall, not brief staring episodes.

(Option 3). Myoclonic seizures present as sudden, brief jerking movements of muscles, not a blank stare with eyelid fluttering.

(Option 4). Tonic-clonic seizures involve stiffening (tonic phase) followed by rhythmic jerking (clonic phase) and are usually associated with loss of consciousness and postictal confusion, which is not described here.

Educational Objective
Absence seizures are brief generalized seizures characterized by staring spells and eyelid fluttering lasting a few seconds, with rapid recovery and no postictal phase.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Seizure Disorders

answerChoiceList.choice: Atonic seizures
Absence seizures
Myoclonic seizures
Tonic-clonic seizures

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides home care instructions to the mother of a child who has undergone myringotomy with the insertion of tympanostomy tubes. Which statement by the mother indicates a need for further instruction?
questionList.explanationText:
Myringotomy with tympanostomy tube insertion is a surgical procedure performed to relieve middle ear pressure and allow drainage of fluid from the middle ear, commonly used in children with recurrent otitis media. The tubes help maintain ventilation of the middle ear and prevent fluid accumulation. After the procedure, proper home care is essential to prevent infection, displacement of tubes, and complications. A fever is not expected after this procedure and may indicate infection or complication. Parents should also be instructed to keep the ears dry, avoid activities that increase pressure (like nose blowing), and report unusual symptoms promptly.

(Correct Option 1). The statement "A fever is normal after this procedure" indicates a need for further instruction because fever is not expected and may signal infection or postoperative complication. The parent should be instructed to notify the healthcare provider if fever occurs.

(Option 2). It is appropriate to notify the doctor if the tubes fall out, as this may affect treatment and require follow-up evaluation.

(Option 3). Keeping the child’s ears dry is correct because water entering the ear can increase the risk of infection.

(Option 4). Avoiding nose blowing for 7 to 10 days is appropriate because it can increase middle ear pressure and dislodge the tubes.

Educational Objective
After tympanostomy tube placement, parents should monitor for signs of infection such as fever, keep ears dry, and avoid activities that increase ear pressure. Fever is not a normal finding and should be reported.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Myringotomy Care

answerChoiceList.choice: “A fever is normal after this procedure.”
“I need to call the doctor if the tubes fall out.”
“I need to keep his ears dry while he’s taking a bath.”
“I should keep him from blowing his nose for 7 to 10 days.”

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides home care instructions to the parents of a child with acute spasmodic croup. The nurse should tell the parents that if stridor at rest occurs, they should:
questionList.explanationText:
Acute spasmodic croup is a condition characterized by laryngeal inflammation, airway narrowing, and upper airway obstruction, often leading to a distinctive barking cough and inspiratory stridor. Stridor at rest is a critical clinical sign indicating severe airway compromise and potential respiratory distress. When the airway becomes significantly narrowed, the child is at risk of hypoxia, respiratory fatigue, and possible respiratory failure. Prompt medical intervention is required in such cases, as worsening obstruction can progress rapidly and become life-threatening.

(Correct Option 2). Taking the child to the emergency department is correct because stridor at rest indicates severe airway obstruction requiring immediate evaluation and treatment. The child may need nebulized epinephrine, corticosteroids, or airway support. Delaying care can lead to respiratory compromise and deterioration, making emergency intervention essential.

(Option 1). Administering an analgesic is incorrect because pain is not the primary issue in croup. The main concern is airway obstruction and respiratory distress, not discomfort, so analgesics will not address the underlying problem.

(Option 3). Placing a cool-mist humidifier may help in mild cases of croup, as moist air can soothe inflamed airways. However, it is insufficient when stridor occurs at rest, which indicates a severe condition requiring emergency care.

(Option 4). Providing steam from hot running water may offer temporary relief in mild to moderate symptoms, but it is not appropriate when severe airway obstruction is present. Relying on this method could delay critical treatment and worsen outcomes.

Educational Objective
Stridor at rest in a child with croup signals severe airway obstruction and requires immediate emergency care. Home remedies like humidified air are only appropriate for mild cases and should not delay urgent medical evaluation.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Croup and Airway Disorders

answerChoiceList.choice: Administer an analgesic
Take the child to the emergency department
Place a cool-mist humidifier in the child’s room
Provide mist from steam produced by hot running water in a closed bathroom

questionList.questionText: A cardiac catheterization is performed on an infant. After the procedure, the nurse should tell the mother that the infant:
questionList.explanationText:
Cardiac catheterization is an invasive diagnostic procedure used to evaluate cardiac anatomy and function. After the procedure, there is a risk of bleeding, hematoma formation, and vascular compromise at the catheter insertion site, usually the femoral artery. In infants, maintaining hemostasis and preventing stress or excessive movement is essential. However, unlike older children or adults, infants can be safely comforted while still maintaining proper positioning. Holding the infant in a position that avoids pressure on the insertion site while promoting comfort helps reduce crying and agitation, which can increase intrathoracic pressure and risk of bleeding.

(Correct Option 2). Holding the infant in a prone position on the mother’s lap is correct because it allows the infant to be comforted while maintaining pressure off the catheter insertion site. This position helps minimize movement and crying, thereby reducing the risk of bleeding or complications. It also supports bonding and decreases infant distress.

(Option 1). Stating that the infant needs to remain in the crib for 6 hours is incorrect because immobility does not require complete separation from the caregiver. Infants can be held safely as long as proper positioning is maintained to protect the insertion site.

(Option 3). Restraining the affected leg for 8 hours is incorrect because prolonged restraint can cause discomfort and is not routinely required in infants. Gentle immobilization is sufficient, and excessive restraint may increase agitation and risk of complications.

(Option 4). Maintaining a 20-degree head-elevated position is incorrect because specific head elevation is not the primary concern. The focus should be on keeping the insertion site stable and avoiding pressure or bending at the site, not on head positioning.

Educational Objective
After cardiac catheterization in infants, maintaining hemostasis and minimizing movement at the insertion site is critical. Infants can be safely held in positions that avoid pressure on the site, promoting comfort and reducing complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Cardiac Catheterization Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Needs to remain in the crib for 6 hours
Can be held in a prone position on the mother’s lap
Needs to have the affected leg restrained for 8 hours
Will have to remain in a 20-degree head-elevated position for several hours

questionList.questionText: A nurse is developing a plan of care for a child at risk for seizures. Which interventions should be carried out if a seizure occurs?
questionList.explanationText:
Seizures are caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain, leading to involuntary muscle movements, altered consciousness, and potential airway compromise. During a seizure, the primary goals of nursing care are to ensure patient safety, maintain airway patency, and prevent injury. The child may experience loss of protective reflexes, increased secretions, and risk of aspiration. Therefore, appropriate positioning and observation are critical while avoiding harmful interventions such as restraint or inserting objects into the mouth, which can lead to trauma or airway obstruction.

(Correct Options 1, 2, and 4). Turning the child on the side is correct because it helps maintain airway patency and prevents aspiration of secretions. Monitoring the child’s movements is essential to assess seizure characteristics, duration, and progression, which guides further management. Loosening clothing around the neck helps reduce airway restriction and improves breathing, ensuring adequate oxygenation during the episode.

(Option 3). Restraining the child’s arms and legs is incorrect because it can cause musculoskeletal injury or worsen agitation. During seizures, movements should not be forcibly restricted; instead, the nurse should focus on protecting the child from injury by clearing the environment.

(Option 5). Inserting a padded tongue blade is incorrect because it can cause dental damage, soft tissue injury, or airway obstruction. It is a common misconception; nothing should be placed in the mouth during a seizure due to the risk of harm.

Educational Objective
During a seizure, nursing interventions focus on airway protection, injury prevention, and monitoring. The child should be placed on the side, clothing loosened, and movements observed, while avoiding restraints and insertion of objects into the mouth.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Seizure Management

answerChoiceList.choice: Turning the child on her side
Monitoring the child’s movements
Restraining the child’s arms and legs
Loosening the clothing around the child’s neck
Gently inserting a padded tongue blade between the child’s upper and lower teeth

questionList.questionText: A child being seen in the clinic is found to have rubeola (measles), and the father asks the nurse how to care for the child. The nurse should tell the father that he should:
questionList.explanationText:
Rubeola (measles) is a highly contagious viral infection characterized by fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and a maculopapular rash. One of the hallmark features of measles is photophobia (sensitivity to light) due to conjunctival inflammation. The child may also experience irritability, malaise, and discomfort. Supportive care focuses on reducing environmental stress, preventing complications, and managing symptoms. Providing a calm, low-stimulation environment helps alleviate discomfort, especially in relation to eye sensitivity.

(Correct Option 1). Keeping the child in a room with dim lights is correct because measles often causes photophobia due to conjunctivitis. Bright light can worsen eye discomfort and irritability, so a dimly lit environment promotes comfort and reduces distress.

(Option 2). Giving warm baths is incorrect because warm water may increase skin irritation and discomfort. Cool or tepid baths are generally preferred if needed for comfort, but bathing is not the primary intervention for measles care.

(Option 3). Allowing the child to play outdoors is incorrect because sunlight can worsen photophobia and discomfort. Additionally, measles is highly contagious, so the child should be isolated to prevent transmission to others.

(Option 4). Administering baby aspirin is incorrect because giving aspirin to children with viral infections can lead to Reye’s syndrome, a potentially life-threatening condition causing acute encephalopathy and liver dysfunction. Alternative antipyretics like acetaminophen are safer.

Educational Objective
Measles management is primarily supportive, focusing on comfort and prevention of complications. A dimly lit environment helps relieve photophobia, and aspirin should be avoided due to the risk of Reye’s syndrome.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Measles (Rubeola)

answerChoiceList.choice: Keep the child in a room with dim lights
Give the child warm baths to help prevent itching
Allow the child to play outdoors, because sunlight will help heal the rash
Take the child’s temperature every 4 hours and administer 1 baby aspirin for fever

questionList.questionText: The nurse is providing home care instructions to the parents of a child with immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) whose platelet count is 19,500 cells/mm³. Which statements by the parents indicate that they understand the instructions?
questionList.explanationText:
Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) is a disorder characterized by immune-mediated destruction of platelets, resulting in thrombocytopenia and increased risk of bleeding. A platelet count of 19,500 cells/mm³ is critically low, placing the child at high risk for spontaneous bleeding, bruising, petechiae, and hemorrhage. Nursing management focuses on preventing injury, avoiding medications that impair platelet function, and monitoring for bleeding. Parents must be educated on safety precautions and early signs of complications to ensure prompt intervention.

(Correct Options 3, 4, and 5). Avoiding aspirin is correct because aspirin inhibits platelet aggregation, increasing the risk of bleeding. Using an extra-soft toothbrush is appropriate to prevent gum trauma and bleeding. Monitoring for signs of bleeding, such as petechiae, bruising, or bleeding gums, is essential for early detection of complications and timely intervention.

(Option 1). Using Motrin (ibuprofen) is incorrect because nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) also impair platelet function and increase bleeding risk. These medications should be avoided in children with thrombocytopenia.

(Option 2). Allowing the child to ride a bike is incorrect because activities with a high risk of injury or falls should be avoided. Trauma can lead to serious bleeding complications, especially with such a low platelet count.

Educational Objective
In ITP, care focuses on preventing bleeding and avoiding platelet-inhibiting medications. Safe practices include using soft hygiene tools, monitoring for bleeding, and restricting high-risk activities.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP)

answerChoiceList.choice: “We’ll use Motrin if he has a fever.”
“It’s all right to let him ride his bike.”
“We need to avoid giving him aspirin.”
“He should use an extra-soft toothbrush.”
“We need to watch for signs of bleeding.”

questionList.questionText: A nurse is conducting a neurovascular assessment of a child who has just had a cast applied to her leg. The nurse notes that the capillary refill time distal to the cast is 4 seconds. In light of this finding, which action by the nurse is appropriate?
questionList.explanationText:
Neurovascular assessment is essential after cast application to evaluate circulation, sensation, and motor function distal to the affected area. A normal capillary refill time is less than 2 seconds. A refill time of 4 seconds indicates impaired peripheral perfusion, which may result from cast tightness, swelling, or developing compartment syndrome. Compartment syndrome is a serious condition characterized by increased pressure within a closed muscle compartment, leading to decreased blood flow, ischemia, and potential tissue necrosis. Early recognition and prompt intervention are critical to prevent permanent damage.

(Correct Option 2). Contacting the healthcare provider is correct because a prolonged capillary refill time is an abnormal and concerning finding. It indicates possible compromised circulation, requiring immediate evaluation and intervention to prevent complications such as compartment syndrome or tissue ischemia.

(Option 1). Documenting the findings alone is incorrect because although documentation is important, it does not address the urgent clinical problem. Immediate action is required when circulation is compromised.

(Option 3). Removing pillows under the leg is incorrect because although elevation can affect circulation, this intervention alone does not resolve potential vascular compromise. The priority is to notify the provider for further assessment.

(Option 4). Continuing routine neurovascular assessments is incorrect because the finding is already abnormal. Waiting without intervention could delay treatment and worsen the condition.

Educational Objective
A prolonged capillary refill time after cast application indicates impaired circulation and requires immediate notification of the healthcare provider to prevent serious complications like compartment syndrome.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Neurovascular Assessment and Cast Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Documenting the findings
Contacting the healthcare provider
Removing any pillows that were placed under the leg
Continuing neurovascular assessments every 1 to 2 hours

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides home care instructions to the mother of a child with pediculosis capitis (head lice). Which statement by the mother indicates a need for further instruction?
questionList.explanationText:
Pediculosis capitis (head lice) is an infestation caused by Pediculus humanus capitis, which lives on the scalp and lays eggs (nits) on hair shafts. Transmission occurs through direct head-to-head contact or sharing personal items. Management focuses on topical pediculicides, mechanical removal of nits, and environmental cleaning. It is important to note that environmental insecticide sprays are not recommended because they are ineffective, unnecessary, and potentially toxic. Proper hygiene measures such as washing linens and disinfecting personal items are sufficient to prevent reinfestation.

(Correct Option 2). The statement about using an antilice spray on the child and surroundings indicates a need for further instruction because environmental insecticide sprays are not recommended. These sprays can be toxic if inhaled or absorbed and do not significantly improve treatment outcomes. Proper treatment involves medicated shampoos and cleaning of personal items, not chemical spraying.

(Option 1). Washing clothes and bedding in hot water and drying on a hot setting is correct because heat effectively kills lice and nits. This is an appropriate environmental control measure.

(Option 3). Boiling or soaking combs and brushes in hot water or medicated solution is correct because it helps eliminate lice and nits from personal grooming items, preventing reinfestation.

(Option 4). Applying petrolatum to eyelashes is correct for managing lice in that area because it helps suffocate lice and facilitates removal. This is a recommended and safe method for delicate areas like eyelashes.

Educational Objective
Management of head lice focuses on topical treatment and hygiene measures. Environmental insecticide sprays should be avoided due to toxicity and lack of effectiveness.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Pediculosis Capitis (Head Lice)

answerChoiceList.choice: “I need to wash her clothes and bedding in hot water and dry them on a hot setting.”
“I need to use an antilice spray on her and on anything that she’s been in contact with.”
“I need to boil or soak her combs and brushes in antilice shampoo or hot water for at least 10 minutes.”
“I can get the lice and nits off her eyelashes by applying petrolatum to the eyelashes twice a day for 8 days.”

questionList.questionText: Which pediatric client is at least risk for otitis media?
questionList.explanationText:
Otitis media is an infection or inflammation of the middle ear, commonly seen in children due to their short, horizontal Eustachian tubes, which allow pathogens to enter easily. Risk factors include bottle-feeding (especially in a supine position), exposure to environmental smoke, and attendance at daycare centers, which increase exposure to pathogens. Breastfeeding, however, provides protective antibodies (IgA) that enhance the child’s immune response and reduce the risk of infections, including otitis media.

(Correct Option 1). A breastfed infant is at least risk because breast milk contains immunoglobulins and antibodies that protect against respiratory and ear infections. Breastfeeding also promotes better Eustachian tube function and reduces exposure to pathogens compared to bottle-feeding.

(Option 2). A bottle-fed infant is at higher risk because feeding in a supine position can allow formula to enter the Eustachian tube, increasing the likelihood of infection.

(Option 3). A child attending daycare is at increased risk due to frequent exposure to other children and infectious agents, which raises the chance of upper respiratory infections that can lead to otitis media.

(Option 4). Exposure to environmental smoke is a significant risk factor because it can irritate the respiratory tract and impair mucociliary function, making the child more susceptible to infections, including otitis media.

Educational Objective
Breastfeeding provides immune protection against infections, reducing the risk of otitis media, while factors like bottle-feeding, daycare attendance, and smoke exposure increase risk.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Otitis Media

answerChoiceList.choice: A breastfed infant
A bottle-fed infant
A child who attends a daycare center
A child exposed to environmental smoke

questionList.questionText: The mother of a child admitted to the hospital with Kawasaki disease asks the nurse about the disease. The nurse responds that it is:
questionList.explanationText:
Kawasaki disease is an acute systemic vasculitis that primarily affects medium-sized arteries, especially the coronary arteries. The disease involves inflammation of the vascular walls, including the smooth muscle cells, leading to edema, necrosis, and potential aneurysm formation. The exact cause is unknown, but it is believed to involve an abnormal immune response in genetically susceptible children. It is not considered a communicable disease and is most common in children under 5 years of age.

(Correct Option 3). Kawasaki disease affects the smooth muscle cells of the vascular walls, causing inflammation and damage to blood vessels. This explains why complications such as coronary artery aneurysms can develop if not treated promptly.

(Option 1). It is incorrect because Kawasaki disease is not a communicable disease. It cannot be transmitted from one person to another.

(Option 2). It is incorrect because Kawasaki disease is not caused by exposure to individuals with rheumatic fever. These are two distinct conditions with different etiologies.

(Option 4). It is incorrect because Kawasaki disease is not associated with seasonal exposure like swimming in lakes. Although it may show seasonal trends, it is not linked to environmental exposure in this way.

Educational Objective
Kawasaki disease is a systemic vasculitis affecting medium-sized arteries, particularly the coronary arteries, involving inflammation of vascular smooth muscle cells and requiring prompt treatment to prevent complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Kawasaki Disease

answerChoiceList.choice: A common communicable disease
Caused by exposure to an individual with rheumatic fever
A disease that affects the smooth muscle cells of the vascular walls
A disease that most often occurs in the summer after swimming in a lake

questionList.questionText: Which medication is essential for the nurse to have available before administering an allergy injection to a child?
questionList.explanationText:
Allergy injections (immunotherapy) are used to reduce sensitivity to allergens but carry a risk of systemic allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis. Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction characterized by airway edema, bronchospasm, hypotension, and cardiovascular collapse. Immediate treatment is critical, and the first-line medication is epinephrine, which acts rapidly to vasoconstrict, bronchodilate, and improve cardiac output. Therefore, it is essential that emergency medications be readily available before administering allergy injections.

(Correct Option 3). Epinephrine is the correct answer because it is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. It works by reversing airway constriction, reducing edema, and stabilizing blood pressure. Having epinephrine immediately available ensures prompt response to any severe allergic reaction.

(Option 1). Immune globulin is incorrect because it is used for passive immunity and certain immune deficiencies, not for emergency management of allergic reactions.

(Option 2). Ibuprofen is incorrect because it is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used for pain and fever, and it does not treat allergic reactions or anaphylaxis.

(Option 4). Acetaminophen is incorrect because it is an analgesic and antipyretic and has no role in managing acute allergic or anaphylactic reactions.

Educational Objective
Epinephrine is the drug of choice for anaphylaxis and must be readily available when administering allergy injections to promptly manage potential life-threatening reactions.
questionList.subject: Pharmacology
questionList.title: Anaphylaxis and Emergency Medications

answerChoiceList.choice: Immune globulin
Ibuprofen (Motrin)
Epinephrine (Adrenalin)
Acetaminophen (Tylenol)

questionList.questionText: The nurse is caring for a 3-year-old with leukemia. The child is not eating and is losing weight as a result of nausea and mucositis stemming from the chemotherapy. Which interventions are appropriate?
questionList.explanationText:
Chemotherapy-induced mucositis is a common complication in children with leukemia, characterized by inflammation, ulceration, and pain of the oral mucosa. This condition leads to difficulty eating, decreased oral intake, and weight loss. Additionally, chemotherapy can cause nausea and altered taste sensation, further reducing appetite. Nursing interventions focus on pain relief, maintaining nutritional intake, and preventing further irritation of the oral mucosa. Providing foods that are easy to swallow, non-irritating, and high in nutritional value is essential to support the child’s recovery and growth.

(Correct Options 1, 4, and 5). Providing small, frequent high-protein foods is appropriate because it helps maximize caloric intake despite poor appetite. Offering cool liquids and soft foods at room temperature reduces oral discomfort and irritation. Applying a prescribed solution of diphenhydramine and Maalox helps soothe the mucosa and provide local relief, improving the child’s ability to eat.

(Option 2). Administering oral viscous lidocaine is incorrect because it can numb the gag reflex and increase the risk of aspiration, especially in young children. Its use is generally avoided or used with caution in pediatric patients.

(Option 3). Bringing in favorite foods is incorrect because while it may encourage intake, these foods may be irritating, spicy, or not nutritionally appropriate during mucositis. The focus should be on soft, bland, and non-irritating foods.

Educational Objective
Management of chemotherapy-induced mucositis focuses on pain relief and nutritional support. Appropriate interventions include soft, non-irritating foods, small frequent meals, and topical treatments to soothe oral discomfort.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Chemotherapy Side Effects and Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Providing small, frequent high-protein foods
Administering oral viscous lidocaine before meals
Having the parents bring in the child’s favorite foods
Providing cool liquids and soft foods at room temperature
Applying a solution of Benadryl (diphenhydramine) and Maalox as prescribed to the mouth

questionList.questionText: A child has been in the hospital for several days for treatment of severe vomiting related to his HIV-positive status. Which assessment finding is the best indication that the child’s condition is improving?
questionList.explanationText:
Severe vomiting in children, especially those with HIV infection, can lead to dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, weight loss, and poor nutritional status. Monitoring improvement involves assessing hydration status, nutritional recovery, and overall physiologic stability. One of the most reliable indicators of recovery is weight gain, as it reflects improved fluid balance, adequate intake, and metabolic recovery. While other signs such as temperature changes or absence of lesions may suggest partial improvement, they do not directly indicate restoration of nutritional and hydration status, which are critical in pediatric recovery.

(Correct Option 2). A weight increase of 1 lb over 3 days is the best indicator of improvement because it reflects positive fluid balance, improved nutritional intake, and resolution of dehydration. In pediatric patients, weight is a highly sensitive and objective measure of clinical progress, especially after episodes of prolonged vomiting. This indicates that the child is retaining fluids and nutrients effectively, signaling recovery.

(Option 1). The absence of lesions in the mouth and throat may indicate some improvement in oral health or opportunistic infections, but it does not directly reflect recovery from vomiting or improvement in hydration and nutritional status.

(Option 3). A decrease in temperature suggests a reduction in fever or infection, but it is not the most reliable indicator of recovery from severe vomiting. It does not confirm adequate fluid and nutritional restoration.

(Option 4). Capillary refill slowing from 2 seconds to 3 seconds is a sign of worsening perfusion and possible dehydration, not improvement. Normal capillary refill is less than 2 seconds, so this finding indicates deterioration rather than recovery.

Educational Objective
In pediatric patients recovering from vomiting, weight gain is the most reliable indicator of improvement, reflecting adequate hydration and nutritional status, while delayed capillary refill signals worsening condition.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: HIV Pediatric Care and Hydration

answerChoiceList.choice: No lesions in the mouth and throat
Weight increase of 1 lb over 3 days
Temperature change from 100.2° F to 99.2° F
Capillary refill slowing from 2 seconds to 3 seconds

questionList.questionText: A nurse is providing home care instructions to the mother of a child with juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Which action should the nurse tell the parents to take during a painful exacerbation?
questionList.explanationText:
Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA) is a chronic inflammatory autoimmune condition characterized by synovial inflammation, leading to joint pain, swelling, stiffness, and decreased mobility. During exacerbations, inflammation increases, causing significant discomfort and reduced function. However, maintaining joint mobility and muscle strength is essential to prevent contractures, muscle atrophy, and long-term disability. Gentle, prescribed range-of-motion and strengthening exercises help preserve joint function while minimizing stiffness. Complete immobilization can worsen outcomes, whereas controlled activity supports circulation, flexibility, and joint integrity.

(Correct Option 4). Encouraging the child to perform the prescribed joint exercises to maintain muscle and joint integrity is correct because regular therapeutic exercise prevents stiffness, maintains range of motion, and reduces long-term complications. Even during painful exacerbations, gentle, guided activity is recommended rather than complete rest.

(Option 1). Splinting the painful joints and avoiding any joint movement is incorrect because prolonged immobilization leads to joint stiffness, muscle wasting, and decreased function. While short-term rest may be used, complete avoidance of movement is harmful.

(Option 2). Encouraging the child to perform simple isometric exercises is partially beneficial but incorrect as the best answer because isometric exercises alone do not provide sufficient joint mobility. A comprehensive program including range-of-motion exercises is required.

(Option 3). Alternating splinting of the painful joints with joint exercises every hour is incorrect because frequent splinting can still contribute to stiffness, and such rigid scheduling is unnecessary. The focus should be on consistent, prescribed exercise routines tailored to the child.

Educational Objective
In juvenile idiopathic arthritis, maintaining joint mobility and muscle strength is critical. Even during exacerbations, children should perform prescribed gentle exercises to prevent stiffness, contractures, and long-term disability.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis

answerChoiceList.choice: Splinting the painful joints and avoiding any joint movement
Encouraging the child to perform simple isometric exercises
Alternating splinting of the painful joints with joint exercises every hour
Encouraging the child to perform the prescribed joint exercises to maintain muscle and joint integrity

questionList.questionText: A nurse is planning diversional activities for a school-age child hospitalized with acute febrile rheumatic fever. Which activity is most appropriate?
questionList.explanationText:
Acute Febrile Rheumatic Fever (ARF) is an inflammatory condition that occurs as a complication of Group A Streptococcal infection, affecting the heart, joints, skin, and central nervous system. During the acute phase, children often experience fever, joint pain (polyarthritis), and carditis, requiring strict bed rest and limited physical activity to reduce cardiac workload and prevent complications. Therefore, activities should be quiet, non-strenuous, and engaging, ensuring minimal energy expenditure while maintaining psychological comfort. Excessive stimulation or physical exertion can worsen symptoms and delay recovery.

(Correct Option 1). Board games are the most appropriate activity because they are quiet, sedentary, and mentally stimulating. They allow the child to engage in play without increasing physical exertion or cardiac stress, which is essential during the acute phase of rheumatic fever.

(Option 2). Twice-daily visits to the playroom are incorrect because ambulation and increased activity can place additional strain on the heart and joints. Children with ARF require bed rest, making playroom visits inappropriate.

(Option 3). Frequent visits from the child’s friends are incorrect because they may cause overstimulation and fatigue. Additionally, infection control concerns exist, as the child is vulnerable during illness.

(Option 4). Visits from other hospitalized children are incorrect due to risk of cross-infection and unnecessary stimulation. Maintaining a controlled and quiet environment is essential for recovery.

Educational Objective
Children with acute rheumatic fever require strict rest and low-stimulation activities. Quiet activities such as board games are ideal, while physical activity and excessive social interaction should be avoided to prevent complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Rheumatic Fever

answerChoiceList.choice: Board games
Twice-daily visits to the playroom
Frequent visits from the child’s friends
Visits from other children who are hospitalized

questionList.questionText: A nurse is planning diversional activities for a school-age child hospitalized with acute febrile rheumatic fever. Which activity is most appropriate?
questionList.explanationText:
Acute Febrile Rheumatic Fever (ARF) is a post-streptococcal inflammatory disease that affects the heart, joints, central nervous system, and skin. The condition often presents with carditis, polyarthritis, fever, and fatigue. During the acute phase, the child requires strict bed rest to minimize cardiac workload and prevent further inflammation or complications. Therefore, activities should be quiet, non-strenuous, and low-energy, ensuring the child remains engaged without increasing physical stress. Overstimulation or excessive movement can worsen symptoms and delay recovery, making sedentary, calm activities the safest option.

(Correct Option 1). Board games are appropriate because they are quiet, sedentary, and mentally stimulating. They allow the child to remain entertained without increasing physical exertion or cardiac demand, which is essential during the acute stage of rheumatic fever.

(Option 2). Twice-daily visits to the playroom are incorrect because they involve physical activity and ambulation, which can increase cardiac workload and worsen the child’s condition. Bed rest is required during the acute phase.

(Option 3). Frequent visits from the child’s friends are incorrect because they may lead to fatigue, overstimulation, and potential exposure to infections, which can complicate recovery.

(Option 4). Visits from other hospitalized children are incorrect due to the risk of cross-infection and increased stimulation. A calm and controlled environment is necessary for healing.

Educational Objective
Children with acute rheumatic fever require strict rest and minimal stimulation. Quiet activities such as board games are ideal, while physical activity and excessive social interaction should be avoided.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever

answerChoiceList.choice: Board games
Twice-daily visits to the playroom
Frequent visits from the child’s friends
Visits from other children who are hospitalized

questionList.questionText: A nurse is conducting an assessment of a 12-year-old with Osgood-Schlatter disease. Which question does the nurse ask the child to elicit data regarding the cause of the disease?
questionList.explanationText:
Osgood-Schlatter disease is an overuse injury commonly seen in adolescents during periods of rapid growth, especially those who are physically active. It involves inflammation of the tibial tubercle due to repeated traction of the patellar tendon on the growth plate. Activities that involve running, jumping, and frequent knee extension increase stress on the developing bone, leading to pain, swelling, and tenderness just below the knee. This condition is not caused by acute trauma or genetic factors but is strongly associated with sports participation and repetitive strain.

(Correct Option 1). Asking “Do you participate in sports?” is correct because repetitive physical activity, particularly sports involving running and jumping, is the primary cause of Osgood-Schlatter disease. This question helps identify the underlying overuse mechanism contributing to the condition.

(Option 2). Asking about falling off a bicycle is incorrect because Osgood-Schlatter disease is not caused by acute trauma. It develops gradually due to repetitive stress rather than a single injury.

(Option 3). Asking about head injury is incorrect because there is no neurological or head-related cause associated with this musculoskeletal condition.

(Option 4). Asking about family history is incorrect because Osgood-Schlatter disease is not primarily hereditary. It is related to activity level and growth patterns rather than genetic predisposition.

Educational Objective
Osgood-Schlatter disease is an overuse injury associated with sports and repetitive knee stress. Assessing participation in physical activities helps identify the cause and guide management.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Osgood-Schlatter Disease

answerChoiceList.choice: “Do you participate in sports?”
“Did you fall off your bicycle?”
“Have you ever fallen and hit your head?”
