“Does anyone else in your family have this disease?”

questionList.questionText: A pediatric community health nurse is conducting a screening program to identify children at risk for a hematologic disorder. The nurse determines that the child at most risk for beta-thalassemia is the child:
questionList.explanationText:
Beta-thalassemia is an inherited hemoglobin disorder caused by a defect in the production of the beta-globin chains of hemoglobin. This results in ineffective erythropoiesis, hemolysis, and chronic anemia. The condition is most prevalent in individuals of Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, African, and Southeast Asian descent due to genetic mutations passed in an autosomal recessive pattern. Reduced beta-globin synthesis leads to an imbalance of globin chains, causing red blood cell destruction and decreased oxygen-carrying capacity. Screening programs focus on identifying individuals with high-risk ethnic backgrounds and family history of hemoglobinopathies.

(Correct Option 1). A child of Mediterranean descent is at greatest risk because beta-thalassemia is highly prevalent in this population. Genetic mutations affecting beta-globin production are commonly inherited in individuals from this region, making ethnicity a key risk factor.

(Option 2). Poor dietary iron intake is incorrect because beta-thalassemia is not caused by iron deficiency. It is a genetic disorder, whereas iron deficiency anemia results from inadequate iron intake.

(Option 3). A known factor VIII deficiency is incorrect because this condition refers to hemophilia A, a clotting disorder, not a hemoglobin synthesis disorder like thalassemia.

(Option 4). Abnormal hemoglobin S (HbS) is incorrect because this is associated with sickle cell disease, a different genetic hemoglobinopathy, not beta-thalassemia.

Educational Objective
Beta-thalassemia is an inherited hemoglobin disorder most common in individuals of Mediterranean descent. Risk assessment focuses on ethnicity and genetic background, not nutritional deficiencies or unrelated hematologic conditions.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Beta-Thalassemia

answerChoiceList.choice: Of Mediterranean descent
Whose dietary intake of iron is poor
Who has a known factor VIII deficiency
Whose parent is known to have abnormal hemoglobin S (HbS)

questionList.questionText: A physician prescribes oral amoxicillin (Amoxil) 60 mg 3 times daily for a child who weighs 12.5 lb. The safe pediatric dosage is 20 to 40 mg/kg/day in 3 equal doses. The medication label reads, “Amoxicillin 125 mg/5 mL.” How many milliliters will the nurse administer per dose?
questionList.explanationText:
Pediatric medication dosing is commonly calculated based on body weight in kilograms to ensure safe and effective drug administration. First, the child’s weight must be converted from pounds to kilograms using the formula (1 kg = 2.2 lb). Next, the prescribed dose is verified against the safe dosage range (mg/kg/day) to ensure it falls within therapeutic limits. The total daily dose is then divided into the prescribed number of doses. Finally, the nurse uses the medication concentration to calculate the volume in milliliters (mL) to administer. In this case, 12.5 lb ÷ 2.2 = approximately 5.7 kg. The prescribed dose (60 mg × 3 = 180 mg/day) falls within the safe range (114–228 mg/day). Using the concentration 125 mg/5 mL, the nurse calculates the required dose per administration.

(Correct Option 3). 2.4 mL is correct because the nurse calculates: 60 mg per dose × (5 mL / 125 mg) = 2.4 mL per dose. This ensures accurate medication delivery based on the prescribed dosage and available concentration.

(Option 1). 1.2 mL is incorrect because this represents half the required dose, which would lead to subtherapeutic treatment and ineffective infection control.

(Option 2). 3 mL is incorrect because this would result in overdosing, administering more than the prescribed 60 mg per dose, potentially increasing the risk of adverse effects.

(Option 4). 5 mL is incorrect because this equals the full 125 mg dose, which is more than double the prescribed amount and unsafe for the child.

Educational Objective
Accurate pediatric dosing requires weight-based calculations, dose verification, and proper conversion using medication concentration. Always ensure the prescribed dose is within the safe mg/kg/day range before administering.
questionList.subject: Pharmacology
questionList.title: Pediatric Dosage Calculation

answerChoiceList.choice: 1.2 mL
3 mL
2.4 mL
5 mL

questionList.questionText: A nurse reviews the prescriptions for a child with Kawasaki disease and notes that the physician has prescribed intravenous immune globulin (IVIG). The nurse should tell the child’s mother that this medication has been prescribed to:
questionList.explanationText:
Kawasaki disease is an acute systemic vasculitis that primarily affects young children and involves inflammation of the medium-sized arteries, especially the coronary arteries. The disease can lead to serious complications such as coronary artery aneurysms, thrombosis, and myocardial infarction if not treated promptly. Intravenous immune globulin (IVIG) is the mainstay of treatment and works by modulating the immune response, reducing inflammation, and decreasing vascular damage. Early administration significantly lowers the risk of coronary artery abnormalities and improves outcomes.

(Correct Option 2). Preventing coronary artery damage is correct because IVIG reduces inflammation in the blood vessels and significantly decreases the risk of coronary artery aneurysm formation, which is the most serious complication of Kawasaki disease.

(Option 1). Reducing fever is incorrect because although IVIG may help reduce fever indirectly, its primary purpose is to prevent cardiovascular complications, not just symptom control.

(Option 3). Alleviating joint pain is incorrect because while joint inflammation can occur, IVIG is not primarily given for pain relief but to address the underlying inflammatory process affecting blood vessels.

(Option 4). Preventing transmission of infection is incorrect because Kawasaki disease is not a contagious illness. IVIG does not function as an antimicrobial or infection control agent.

Educational Objective
IVIG is the primary treatment for Kawasaki disease and is used to reduce vascular inflammation and prevent coronary artery complications, which are the most serious outcomes of the disease.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Kawasaki Disease

answerChoiceList.choice: Reduce the child’s fever
Prevent coronary artery damage
Alleviate pain from joint inflammation
Prevent the transmission of the infection to others

questionList.questionText: A nurse is providing home care instructions to the mother of an infant who has just been found to have hemophilia. The nurse should tell the mother to:
questionList.explanationText:
Hemophilia is a genetic bleeding disorder caused by a deficiency of clotting factors (commonly factor VIII or IX), resulting in impaired blood clotting and prolonged bleeding. Even minor trauma can lead to serious bleeding episodes, including internal hemorrhage and joint bleeding. Therefore, prevention of injury is critical in infants and children. Nursing care focuses on safety measures, avoidance of trauma, and prevention of invasive procedures. Environmental modifications such as padding surfaces help minimize the risk of injury and bleeding complications.

(Correct Option 1). Padding crib rails and table corners is correct because it helps prevent injury and trauma, which are major triggers for bleeding in children with hemophilia. Creating a safe environment is a primary intervention to reduce the risk of hemorrhage.

(Option 2). Using baby aspirin is incorrect because aspirin has antiplatelet effects that can worsen bleeding tendencies. Children with hemophilia should avoid medications that interfere with clotting mechanisms.

(Option 3). Using a soft toothbrush is generally recommended for older children, but it is not the most appropriate instruction for an infant. Additionally, the priority is preventing trauma rather than focusing on dental hygiene at this stage.

(Option 4). Using lubricant for rectal temperature measurement is incorrect because rectal procedures should be avoided in children with hemophilia due to the risk of mucosal injury and bleeding.

Educational Objective
Hemophilia management focuses on preventing injury and bleeding. Environmental safety measures, such as padding furniture and avoiding trauma or invasive procedures, are essential for reducing complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Hemophilia

answerChoiceList.choice: Pad crib rails and table corners
Use baby aspirin for pain relief
Use a soft toothbrush for dental hygiene
Use a generous amount of lubricant when taking the child’s temperature rectally

questionList.questionText: A child has a plaster of Paris cast applied to his arm after fracturing the arm in a fall. The nurse should tell the mother that the cast:
questionList.explanationText:
Plaster of Paris casts are commonly used for immobilizing fractures due to their ability to mold closely to body contours and provide rigid support. However, these casts have specific characteristics that caregivers must understand. They are heavy, not water-resistant, and take a prolonged time to dry. Typically, a plaster cast requires 24 to 48 hours to completely dry and harden, during which time it remains vulnerable to indentation and damage. Proper care includes keeping the cast dry, handling it carefully, and avoiding pressure until fully set to prevent complications such as improper alignment or skin breakdown.

(Correct Option 4). Takes 24 hours or more to dry is correct because plaster of Paris casts require a long drying time (24–48 hours) to fully harden. During this period, the cast must be handled carefully to prevent indentations and weakening.

(Option 1). Is water resistant is incorrect because plaster casts are highly absorbent and can become soft and damaged when wet, increasing the risk of skin irritation and cast breakdown.

(Option 2). Is very lightweight is incorrect because plaster casts are heavier than synthetic casts, making them less comfortable but useful for better molding.

(Option 3). Will quickly dry if it gets wet is incorrect because moisture actually delays drying and weakens the cast structure, potentially compromising immobilization.

Educational Objective
Plaster of Paris casts require 24–48 hours to fully dry and must be kept dry and handled carefully to maintain effectiveness and prevent complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Fracture Management

answerChoiceList.choice: Is water resistant
Is very lightweight
Will quickly dry if it gets wet
Takes 24 hours or more to dry

questionList.questionText: What instruction should the nurse provide to a parent regarding the prevention of urinary tract infection in his child?
questionList.explanationText:
Urinary tract infections (UTIs) in children occur when bacteria, most commonly Escherichia coli, ascend the urethra and infect the bladder or kidneys. Children are particularly susceptible due to shorter urethral length and improper hygiene practices. Risk factors include poor perineal hygiene, urinary stasis, irritation of the urethral area, and tight or non-breathable clothing. Preventive strategies focus on maintaining proper hygiene, adequate fluid intake, frequent voiding, and avoiding irritants such as bubble baths, which can cause urethral irritation and promote bacterial entry.

(Correct Option 2). Avoid giving the child bubble baths is correct because bubble baths can irritate the urethral mucosa and disrupt normal protective barriers, increasing the risk of bacterial entry and infection. Eliminating irritants is an important preventive measure.

(Option 1). Wrapping the diaper tightly is incorrect because tight diapers can trap moisture and promote bacterial growth, increasing the risk of infection rather than preventing it.

(Option 3). Using synthetic fabric underwear is incorrect because such materials retain heat and moisture, creating an environment favorable for bacterial growth. Cotton underwear is preferred.

(Option 4). Encouraging the child to hold urine is incorrect because urinary stasis promotes bacterial multiplication in the bladder, increasing the risk of UTIs. Children should be encouraged to void regularly.

Educational Objective
Preventing UTIs in children involves avoiding irritants, promoting good hygiene, encouraging regular voiding, and using breathable clothing. Bubble baths should be avoided to reduce urethral irritation and infection risk.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Urinary Tract Infection Prevention

answerChoiceList.choice: Wrap the diaper tightly on the child
Avoid giving the child bubble baths
Use underwear made of a synthetic fabric
Encourage the child to hold the urine to avoid frequent voiding

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides home care instructions to the parents of a child who has undergone heart surgery. The nurse should tell the parents to:
questionList.explanationText:
Postoperative care following pediatric heart surgery focuses on monitoring for complications, promoting healing, and ensuring adequate cardiac function. After surgery, children are at risk for infection, heart failure, decreased cardiac output, and poor tissue perfusion. Early warning signs of complications include decreased appetite, fatigue, irritability, fever, and poor weight gain. A decrease in appetite may indicate reduced cardiac output or developing infection, both of which require prompt medical evaluation. Parents must be educated to recognize these warning signs and seek medical attention immediately.

(Correct Option 3). Contact the physician if the child’s appetite decreases is correct because a reduction in appetite can signal cardiac compromise, infection, or decreased oxygenation. Early reporting allows timely intervention and prevents serious complications.

(Option 1). Applying lotion to the incision line is incorrect because lotions should not be applied to a healing surgical incision unless specifically prescribed, as they may increase the risk of infection or delayed healing.

(Option 2). Restricting fluid intake is incorrect unless specifically ordered by the physician. Children recovering from heart surgery typically require adequate hydration to support recovery and maintain circulation.

(Option 4). Allowing the child to resume activities as tolerated is incorrect because activity should be gradually increased and guided by medical advice. Overexertion can place stress on the healing heart and lead to complications.

Educational Objective
After pediatric heart surgery, parents should monitor for early signs of complications, such as decreased appetite, and report them promptly. Early detection helps prevent serious postoperative complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Postoperative Cardiac Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Apply lotion to the incision line twice a day
Restrict fluid intake to prevent fluid retention
Contact the physician if the child’s appetite decreases
Allow the child to resume activities as tolerated by the child

questionList.questionText: The mother of a child with hemophilia calls the clinic nurse and reports that her child has hit his knee on the corner of a coffee table and that the joint appears swollen. The nurse should tell the mother immediately to:
questionList.explanationText:
Hemophilia is a genetic bleeding disorder caused by a deficiency of clotting factors (factor VIII or IX), leading to prolonged bleeding and inability to form stable clots. One of the most common complications is hemarthrosis (bleeding into joints), especially after minor trauma. This results in joint swelling, pain, decreased mobility, and potential long-term joint damage. Immediate management focuses on the RICE protocol (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation), where immobilization (rest) is critical to prevent further bleeding and joint damage. Early intervention helps minimize complications such as chronic joint deformities and disability.

(Correct Option 1). Immobilize the affected joint is correct because resting and immobilizing the joint helps prevent further bleeding into the joint space. This is a key component of the initial management of hemarthrosis and reduces pain, swelling, and risk of long-term joint damage.

(Option 2). Taking the child to the emergency department is not the immediate first action. While medical evaluation may be necessary later, the priority is to initiate immediate first aid (immobilization) at home to control bleeding.

(Option 3). Elevating the joint with a heating pad is incorrect because heat increases blood flow and can worsen bleeding. Instead, cold application (ice) is recommended to reduce bleeding and swelling.

(Option 4). Bringing the child to the primary healthcare provider is not the first priority. Immediate joint protection and bleeding control should be initiated before seeking further evaluation.

Educational Objective
In hemophilia, joint injuries require immediate immobilization and bleeding control to prevent hemarthrosis complications. Avoid heat and prioritize rest and protection of the affected joint.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Hemophilia Management

answerChoiceList.choice: Immobilize the affected joint
Take the child to the emergency department
Elevate the affected joint and apply a heating pad
Bring the child to his primary healthcare provider

questionList.questionText: A child with severe respiratory distress is seen in the emergency department and treated for an acute asthmatic episode. Which assessment finding indicates that the child’s condition is improving?
questionList.explanationText:
Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterized by bronchospasm, airway edema, and mucus production, leading to airflow obstruction. During a severe asthma attack, air movement may be so limited that breath sounds are diminished or absent ("silent chest"), which is a dangerous sign. As treatment begins to work, airways open and airflow improves, resulting in the return or increase of wheezing. Although wheezing is abnormal, its presence after severe obstruction indicates that air is moving through previously constricted airways, signaling clinical improvement.

(Correct Option 3). Increased wheezing is correct because it reflects improved airflow through the bronchi. Initially, severe obstruction may produce minimal or no wheezing, so the return or increase of wheezing indicates that airway patency is improving and the child is responding to treatment.

(Option 1). Stridor is incorrect because it indicates upper airway obstruction, not improvement in asthma. It is a serious sign and unrelated to lower airway bronchospasm.

(Option 2). Shortness of breath is incorrect because it reflects ongoing respiratory distress and poor oxygenation, not clinical improvement.

(Option 4). Dyspnea on exertion is incorrect because it still indicates limited respiratory function and incomplete recovery from the asthmatic episode.

Educational Objective
In acute asthma, increased wheezing after treatment indicates improving airflow. A silent chest is more concerning, while the return of wheezing suggests bronchodilation and clinical improvement.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Asthma Management

answerChoiceList.choice: Stridor
Shortness of breath
Increased wheezing
Dyspnea on exertion

questionList.questionText: A child with severe respiratory distress is seen in the emergency department and treated for an acute asthmatic episode. Which assessment finding indicates that the child’s condition is improving?
questionList.explanationText:
Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways characterized by bronchoconstriction, airway edema, and increased mucus production, leading to airflow limitation and respiratory distress. In severe asthma exacerbations, airflow may become so restricted that breath sounds are diminished or absent ("silent chest"), indicating critical obstruction. As treatment with bronchodilators and anti-inflammatory medications takes effect, airways begin to open, allowing air to move through previously constricted bronchi. This results in the return or increase of wheezing sounds, which paradoxically indicates improving ventilation and airflow.

(Correct Option 3). Increased wheezing is correct because it signifies improved airflow through partially opened airways. In severe asthma, lack of wheezing can indicate minimal airflow, so the presence or increase of wheezing shows that the child is responding to treatment and airway patency is improving.

(Option 1). Stridor is incorrect because it indicates upper airway obstruction (such as croup or foreign body), not improvement in lower airway conditions like asthma.

(Option 2). Shortness of breath is incorrect because it reflects ongoing respiratory distress and inadequate oxygenation, indicating that the condition has not yet improved.

(Option 4). Dyspnea on exertion is incorrect because it still represents limited respiratory function and incomplete recovery, rather than improvement in the acute phase.

Educational Objective
In acute asthma management, the return or increase of wheezing indicates improved airflow. A silent chest is more concerning, while wheezing suggests effective bronchodilation and clinical improvement.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Asthma Management

answerChoiceList.choice: Stridor
Shortness of breath
Increased wheezing
Dyspnea on exertion

questionList.questionText: A nurse is reviewing the physician’s preoperative prescriptions for a child who is scheduled for an appendectomy. Which prescription should the nurse question?
questionList.explanationText:
Appendicitis is an acute inflammation of the appendix that can progress to perforation if the appendix ruptures. In children with suspected appendicitis, nursing care focuses on preventing rupture and limiting anything that could increase intra-abdominal pressure. Preoperative management commonly includes monitoring vital signs, maintaining intravenous fluid access, and providing comfort measures such as a cold pack. Procedures that stimulate bowel activity, such as laxatives or enemas, are avoided because they may increase intestinal motility and pressure, raising the risk of perforation and worsening peritonitis.

(Correct Option 3). Administering a Fleet enema before surgery is contraindicated in a child with appendicitis. An enema increases bowel motility and intraluminal pressure, which can precipitate appendiceal rupture. Because rupture can lead to peritonitis, sepsis, and other serious complications, this is the prescription the nurse should question immediately before the appendectomy.

(Option 1). Checking vital signs every hour is an appropriate preoperative prescription. Frequent monitoring helps the nurse detect fever, tachycardia, or other signs that may indicate worsening inflammation, perforation, or developing infection while the child awaits surgery.

(Option 2). Inserting an intravenous (IV) line is appropriate for a child scheduled for appendectomy. IV access is needed to maintain hydration, especially because the child is typically kept NPO, and it also allows administration of fluids, antibiotics, and medications as prescribed.

(Option 4). Applying a cold pack to the abdomen as needed for comfort is acceptable. Cold may help reduce discomfort and local inflammation, whereas heat is avoided because it can increase circulation to the area and potentially contribute to rupture.

Educational Objective
In suspected or confirmed appendicitis, enemas, laxatives, and heat to the abdomen are avoided because they may increase the risk of appendiceal rupture. Safe preoperative care includes monitoring vital signs, maintaining IV access, keeping the child NPO, and using comfort measures such as a cold pack if prescribed.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Appendicitis and Preoperative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Check vital signs every hour.
Insert an intravenous (IV) line.
Administer a Fleet enema before surgery.
Apply a cold pack to the abdomen as needed for comfort.

questionList.questionText: A nurse is providing home care instructions to the mother of a child who has undergone cleft lip repair. Which statements by the mother indicate an understanding of these instructions? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Cleft lip repair is a surgical procedure aimed at correcting a congenital defect in the lip, and postoperative care focuses on protecting the suture line, preventing infection, and promoting proper healing. The surgical site is delicate, and any trauma, pressure, or contamination can lead to complications such as wound dehiscence or infection. Oral hygiene must be maintained carefully without disturbing the sutures, and feeding practices should minimize irritation. Parents are also educated to monitor for signs of infection such as fever, redness, swelling, or drainage. Positioning and use of devices must avoid placing stress on the repaired lip.

(Correct Option 2). Avoiding brushing the child’s teeth for 1 to 2 weeks is correct because brushing can disrupt the surgical repair site and damage sutures. Instead, gentle cleansing methods such as rinsing are recommended until healing progresses.

(Correct Option 3). Rinsing the mouth with water after feeding is appropriate because it helps maintain oral hygiene and prevents accumulation of milk or formula near the incision, reducing the risk of infection.

(Correct Option 4). Monitoring for signs of infection such as drainage, redness, swelling, or fever is essential. Early detection of infection allows prompt treatment and helps prevent complications that could impair healing.

(Option 1). Placing the infant on the stomach to sleep is incorrect. Infants should be placed on their back (supine position) to reduce the risk of Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS) and to avoid unnecessary pressure on the surgical site.

(Option 5). The statement about never using a bulb syringe is incorrect. A bulb syringe may be used carefully if needed to clear secretions, but it must be done gently to avoid trauma to the surgical area. Complete avoidance is not necessary, only caution is required.

Educational Objective
After cleft lip repair, care focuses on protecting the suture line, maintaining gentle oral hygiene, and monitoring for infection. Parents should avoid actions that place pressure on the lip while ensuring cleanliness and early detection of complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Cleft Lip Postoperative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: “I should put her on her stomach to sleep.”
“I shouldn’t brush her teeth for 1 to 2 weeks.”
“I should rinse her mouth with water after feeding her.”
“I should watch signs of infection like drainage or fever.”
“I should never use a bulb syringe to clear secretions from her mouth.”

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for an infant with Hirschsprung’s disease. Which manifestation of the disease should the nurse expect to see?
questionList.explanationText:
Hirschsprung’s disease is a congenital disorder characterized by the absence of ganglion cells in segments of the colon, leading to impaired peristalsis and functional bowel obstruction. Because the affected segment cannot relax, stool accumulates proximal to the obstruction, causing abdominal distention, constipation, and characteristic stool patterns. The stagnation of fecal matter often results in foul-smelling stools that may appear narrow or ribbon-like due to passage through the constricted segment. Infants may also present with delayed passage of meconium, vomiting, and signs of enterocolitis if untreated.

(Correct Option 2). Foul-smelling, ribbon-like stools are a hallmark finding in Hirschsprung’s disease. The absence of normal nerve innervation leads to a narrowed distal colon, causing stool to pass in thin, ribbon-like shapes. The accumulation and stagnation of stool also contribute to the strong odor, making this manifestation characteristic of the disease.

(Option 1). Non-bilious projectile vomiting is more commonly associated with pyloric stenosis, not Hirschsprung’s disease. In Hirschsprung’s disease, vomiting may occur but is not typically projectile or specifically non-bilious.

(Option 3). A sausage-shaped abdominal mass is characteristic of intussusception, where a segment of the intestine telescopes into another. This finding is not associated with Hirschsprung’s disease.

(Option 4). Bloody, mucousy “currant jelly” stools are also a classic sign of intussusception, resulting from bowel ischemia and sloughing of mucosa. This is not seen in Hirschsprung’s disease.

Educational Objective
Hirschsprung’s disease involves absence of ganglion cells, leading to functional bowel obstruction. Key manifestations include chronic constipation, abdominal distention, and foul-smelling, ribbon-like stools due to passage through a narrowed colon segment.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Hirschsprung’s Disease

answerChoiceList.choice: Non-bilious projectile vomiting
Foul-smelling, ribbon-like stools
A sausage-shaped abdominal mass
Bloody, mucousy “currant jelly” stools

questionList.questionText: A nurse reviews a child’s record and notes documentation that the child is obtunded. On the basis of this documentation, which of the following findings would the nurse expect to note while conducting a neurological assessment?
questionList.explanationText:
Levels of consciousness (LOC) are important indicators of neurological function and are commonly described using terms such as alert, lethargic, obtunded, stuporous, and comatose. An obtunded state represents a moderate reduction in alertness where the patient has a decreased level of responsiveness and requires stimulation to become aroused. Once aroused, the individual may show limited interaction with the environment and may quickly fall back asleep. This state is more severe than lethargy but less severe than stupor or coma, and it indicates possible central nervous system dysfunction that requires close monitoring.

(Correct Option 4). The statement that the child sleeps unless aroused and, once aroused, has limited interaction with the environment accurately describes an obtunded level of consciousness. These patients are difficult to fully awaken and do not maintain sustained alertness or meaningful interaction, which is consistent with this neurological state.

(Option 1). Requiring considerable stimulation to be aroused describes a stuporous state, which is a more severe level of impaired consciousness than obtunded. In stupor, the patient responds only to vigorous or painful stimuli.

(Option 2). Awakening easily but exhibiting limited responsiveness is more consistent with a lethargic state. In lethargy, the patient is drowsy but can be easily aroused and is able to respond, though responses may be slow.

(Option 3). Being awake, alert, oriented, and interactive reflects a normal level of consciousness. This does not align with an obtunded state, which involves decreased alertness and responsiveness.

Educational Objective
An obtunded level of consciousness is characterized by decreased alertness, the need for stimulation to awaken, and limited interaction once aroused. Recognizing differences between LOC levels helps nurses detect neurological deterioration early.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Levels of Consciousness

answerChoiceList.choice: The child requires considerable stimulation to be aroused.
The child awakens easily but exhibits limited responsiveness.
The child is awake, alert, oriented, and interacts with the environment.
The child sleeps unless aroused and, once aroused, has limited interaction with the environment.

questionList.questionText: A nurse has provided dietary instructions to the mother of a child with Crohn’s disease. Which statements by the mother indicate an understanding of the instructions? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Crohn’s disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) characterized by inflammation that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract, leading to malabsorption, nutritional deficiencies, and increased metabolic needs. Children with Crohn’s disease often require a high-calorie, high-protein diet to support growth and healing. Foods that are high in fiber, such as raw vegetables, nuts, and seeds, can irritate the inflamed bowel and worsen symptoms. Additionally, due to malabsorption, children may require vitamin and mineral supplementation, including iron, to prevent deficiencies and anemia.

(Correct Option 2). Avoiding high-fiber vegetables like corn is correct because these foods can be difficult to digest and may irritate the inflamed intestinal mucosa. Limiting fiber intake during active disease helps reduce symptoms such as diarrhea and abdominal pain.

(Correct Option 3). Snacks such as nuts can provide additional protein and calories, which are essential for children with Crohn’s disease who often experience weight loss and growth delays. However, tolerance should be monitored, and foods should be adjusted based on symptom severity.

(Correct Option 5). Taking a multivitamin and iron supplement is appropriate because children with Crohn’s disease are at risk for nutritional deficiencies, including iron-deficiency anemia, due to malabsorption and chronic inflammation.

(Option 1). Including meat in the diet is generally beneficial because it provides protein, but this statement alone does not specifically demonstrate understanding of dietary restrictions or disease-specific modifications. It is not the best indicator compared to other options.

(Option 4). Giving ice cream every day is incorrect. Dairy products may not be well tolerated due to lactose intolerance often seen in Crohn’s disease, and excessive intake can worsen diarrhea and gastrointestinal discomfort.

Educational Objective
Dietary management of Crohn’s disease includes a high-calorie, high-protein diet, avoidance of high-fiber or irritating foods, and use of vitamin and mineral supplements to address malabsorption and prevent deficiencies.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Crohn’s Disease Dietary Management

answerChoiceList.choice: “It’s important to include meat in his diet.”
“I won’t give him high-fiber vegetables like corn.”
“Snacks such as nuts will help provide the extra protein he needs.”
“I should give him ice cream every day to be sure that he gets his calcium.”
“I’ll make sure that he takes a multivitamin and iron supplement every day.”

questionList.questionText: Intravenous potassium chloride in 0.9% sodium chloride solution has been prescribed for a child who is severely dehydrated. Before administering the solution, the nurse must:
questionList.explanationText:
Potassium chloride (KCl) is a critical electrolyte used to treat hypokalemia, but it must be administered with extreme caution because excessive potassium can lead to life-threatening hyperkalemia and cardiac arrhythmias. The kidneys are primarily responsible for potassium excretion, so adequate renal function must be confirmed before administering potassium. In a dehydrated child, renal perfusion may be compromised, increasing the risk of potassium accumulation. Therefore, ensuring that the child has adequate urine output is essential before giving IV potassium to prevent toxicity and complications such as cardiac arrest.

(Correct Option 1). Checking urine output is the most important step before administering potassium. Adequate urine output indicates that the kidneys are functioning properly and can excrete excess potassium. If urine output is low or absent, potassium administration can result in dangerous accumulation and severe hyperkalemia, making this assessment critical for patient safety.

(Option 2). Evaluating skin turgor helps assess the degree of dehydration but does not provide information about renal function or the body’s ability to excrete potassium. Therefore, it is not the priority before administering potassium.

(Option 3). Measuring capillary refill is useful in assessing perfusion and hydration status, but it does not indicate whether the kidneys are functioning adequately to handle potassium excretion.

(Option 4). Obtaining blood pressure is important in evaluating overall hemodynamic status, but it does not directly assess kidney function or potassium excretion capability, which is the key concern before administering potassium.

Educational Objective
Before administering IV potassium, the nurse must always verify adequate urine output to ensure proper renal function and prevent hyperkalemia, a potentially life-threatening condition.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Electrolyte Management - Potassium

answerChoiceList.choice: Check urine output
Evaluate skin turgor
Measure capillary refill
Obtain the child’s blood pressure

questionList.questionText: A nurse is reviewing the chart of a child with a brain tumor. Which symptom would the nurse expect to note in the history and physical?
questionList.explanationText:
Brain tumors in children often produce symptoms related to increased intracranial pressure (ICP) and compression of surrounding brain structures. As the tumor grows, it can obstruct the normal flow of cerebrospinal fluid or occupy space within the skull, causing pressure to rise. Classic manifestations of increased ICP include morning headache, vomiting, changes in behavior, visual disturbances, and altered neurological status. Morning symptoms are especially common because lying flat overnight increases intracranial pressure, and the act of getting out of bed can further intensify discomfort. These findings are important because they may indicate worsening neurological compromise and require prompt evaluation and management.

(Correct Option 4). Headache and morning vomiting related to the child’s getting out of bed are classic findings associated with a brain tumor and increased intracranial pressure. The headache is often worse in the morning because intracranial pressure rises during the night while the child is lying flat. Vomiting without preceding nausea may also occur in the morning and is a common early neurological symptom in children with brain tumors.

(Option 1). Nausea that occurs at bedtime is not a typical hallmark symptom of a brain tumor. Symptoms related to increased intracranial pressure are generally more pronounced in the morning, not at bedtime, because of overnight changes in pressure dynamics.

(Option 2). Fatigue after activity is a nonspecific finding and may occur with many childhood illnesses or even normal exertion. It does not specifically point to a brain tumor or increased intracranial pressure.

(Option 3). Dizziness that occurs late in the day is also nonspecific and does not reflect the classic pattern seen with brain tumors. Neurological symptoms of intracranial pressure typically worsen after lying flat overnight, making morning symptoms much more characteristic.

Educational Objective
Children with brain tumors commonly present with signs of increased intracranial pressure, especially morning headache and vomiting. Recognizing this classic pattern helps nurses identify neurological deterioration early and support timely intervention.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Brain Tumor Manifestations

answerChoiceList.choice: Nausea that occurs at bedtime
Fatigue that occurs after activity
Dizziness that occurs late in the day
Headache and morning vomiting related to the child’s getting out of bed

questionList.questionText: The mother of a child with iron-deficiency anemia who is receiving an oral iron supplement calls the nurse and reports that the child is having black stools. Which response by the nurse is appropriate?
questionList.explanationText:
Iron-deficiency anemia is commonly treated with oral iron supplements, which help replenish the body’s iron stores and support hemoglobin production. A well-known side effect of oral iron therapy is the passage of dark green or black stools, which occurs due to the unabsorbed iron being excreted in the feces. This change in stool color is expected and does not indicate gastrointestinal bleeding. However, it is important for nurses to educate caregivers about this harmless side effect to prevent unnecessary concern and to ensure adherence to therapy. Differentiating between normal medication effects and pathological findings such as melena is essential in pediatric care.

(Correct Option 3). Black, tarry stools are a normal finding when oral iron supplements are being administered. The discoloration results from the oxidation of iron in the gastrointestinal tract and is an expected side effect. Reassuring the parent helps promote continued compliance with treatment and reduces anxiety.

(Option 1). Saying “It’s nothing to worry about” is too vague and does not provide appropriate education. The nurse should give a clear explanation that the stool color change is related to the iron supplement and is expected.

(Option 2). Advising the parent to go to the emergency department is unnecessary because black stools are a normal side effect of iron therapy, not a sign of an emergency in this context.

(Option 4). Requesting a stool specimen to check for blood is not needed unless there are other concerning symptoms. The black stool in this case is due to iron excretion, not gastrointestinal bleeding.

Educational Objective
Oral iron supplements commonly cause black or dark stools, which is a normal and expected side effect. Nurses should educate caregivers to distinguish this from signs of gastrointestinal bleeding.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Iron-Deficiency Anemia Management

answerChoiceList.choice: “It’s nothing to worry about.”
“You need to bring the child to the emergency department and have this checked.”
“Black, tarry stools are a normal finding when oral iron supplements are being administered.”
“You need to obtain a stool specimen and bring it to the physician’s office so that we can check it for blood.”

questionList.questionText: A nurse is conducting an assessment on a child admitted with suspected von Willebrand’s disease (VWD). Which question does the nurse ask to elicit information specific to the manifestations associated with this disease?
questionList.explanationText:
Von Willebrand’s disease (VWD) is a hereditary bleeding disorder caused by a deficiency or dysfunction of von Willebrand factor (vWF), a protein essential for platelet adhesion and stabilization of factor VIII. Because vWF plays a critical role in primary hemostasis, patients with VWD experience problems with clot formation, leading to mucocutaneous bleeding. Common manifestations include frequent nosebleeds (epistaxis), easy bruising, prolonged bleeding after injury, and bleeding from gums. Unlike coagulation disorders that cause deep tissue bleeding, VWD typically presents with superficial bleeding symptoms, making targeted history-taking essential for diagnosis.

(Correct Option 4). Asking about the frequency of nosebleeds directly assesses a common manifestation of von Willebrand’s disease. Recurrent epistaxis is a hallmark symptom due to impaired platelet adhesion and fragile mucosal blood vessels, making this question highly relevant for identifying the disorder.

(Option 1). Painful urination is related to urinary tract conditions, not bleeding disorders like VWD. It does not provide information about clotting abnormalities or bleeding tendencies.

(Option 2). Excessive thirst is associated with conditions such as diabetes mellitus or dehydration, not with von Willebrand’s disease or bleeding disorders.

(Option 3). Frequent headaches may be related to neurological or systemic issues but are not a typical manifestation of VWD, which primarily involves mucosal bleeding rather than neurological symptoms.

Educational Objective
Von Willebrand’s disease is characterized by impaired platelet adhesion leading to mucocutaneous bleeding, such as frequent nosebleeds. Focused assessment questions should target these specific bleeding manifestations.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Von Willebrand Disease

answerChoiceList.choice: “Does it hurt to urinate?”
“Are you always thirsty?”
“Do you have frequent headaches?”
“How many times have you had a nosebleed?”

questionList.questionText: A nurse, providing information to the mother of a child with irritable bowel syndrome tells the mother that:
questionList.explanationText:
Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a functional gastrointestinal disorder characterized by abdominal pain, altered bowel habits (such as diarrhea, constipation, or both), and intestinal motility disturbances without structural or biochemical abnormalities. The condition is often associated with stress, dietary triggers, and changes in gut sensitivity. Although IBS is a chronic condition, it does not cause permanent damage to the gastrointestinal tract. Management focuses on symptom control, including dietary modifications, stress reduction, and sometimes medications to regulate bowel function. Education is important to reassure families and promote adherence to individualized treatment plans.

(Correct Option 3). Treatment is aimed at relieving the symptoms is correct. IBS management focuses on controlling symptoms such as pain, diarrhea, or constipation through dietary changes, stress management, and medications if needed. There is no single cure, but many children can achieve good symptom control with appropriate management.

(Option 1). While IBS is a chronic condition with no definitive cure, simply stating “there is no cure” is incomplete and does not emphasize the importance of symptom management. Education should focus on how the condition can be effectively controlled.

(Option 2). Fiber does not need to be completely eliminated; in fact, fiber intake may help regulate bowel function in some patients. Recommendations vary depending on symptoms, and individualized dietary adjustments are required.

(Option 4). Surgery and creation of a permanent colostomy are not indicated for IBS because it is a functional disorder, not a structural disease requiring surgical intervention. This option reflects a misunderstanding of the condition.

Educational Objective
Irritable bowel syndrome is a functional gastrointestinal disorder managed through symptom control, including diet and stress management. There is no structural damage, and treatment focuses on improving quality of life.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Irritable Bowel Syndrome

answerChoiceList.choice: There is no cure
Fiber must be eliminated from the diet
Treatment is aimed at relieving the symptoms
Surgery and creation of a permanent colostomy will be necessary

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides home care instructions to the mother of a child with impetigo. Which statement by the mother indicates the need for further instruction?
questionList.explanationText:
Impetigo is a highly contagious bacterial skin infection commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes. It is characterized by honey-colored crusted lesions, typically on the face, hands, or extremities. The infection spreads through direct contact or contaminated objects such as towels and clothing. Management includes topical or oral antibiotics, maintaining good hygiene, and preventing spread to others. Children with impetigo should be kept home until they have been on antibiotics for at least 24 hours and lesions are no longer actively draining. Proper wound care and infection control measures are essential to limit transmission.

(Correct Option 1). Saying “It’s OK for him to go to school tomorrow” indicates a need for further instruction. Because impetigo is highly contagious, the child should remain at home until receiving antibiotic therapy for at least 24 hours and lesions are controlled. Sending the child to school too early increases the risk of spreading the infection to others.

(Option 2). Wearing gloves while providing care is appropriate because it helps prevent direct contact transmission of the infection. This demonstrates correct understanding of infection control practices.

(Option 3). Avoiding sharing towels or utensils is correct because impetigo can spread through contaminated personal items. This statement reflects proper infection prevention measures.

(Option 4). Soaking and gently removing crusts with a warm, soapy washcloth several times a day is appropriate care. This helps cleanse the lesions, promotes healing, and allows better medication absorption.

Educational Objective
Impetigo is a highly contagious skin infection. Children should remain home until at least 24 hours after starting antibiotics. Proper hygiene, wound care, and avoiding shared items help prevent transmission.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Impetigo Management

answerChoiceList.choice: “It’s OK for him to go to school tomorrow.”
“I need to wear gloves while I’m taking care of him.”
“My husband and I shouldn’t share towels or utensils with him.”
“I need to soak the crusts and then wash them off with a warm, soapy washcloth three times a day.”

questionList.questionText: A nurse is monitoring a child who sustained a head injury. Which assessment finding is an early sign of increased intracranial pressure (ICP)?
questionList.explanationText:
Increased intracranial pressure (ICP) occurs when the volume of brain tissue, cerebrospinal fluid, or blood within the skull increases, leading to compression of brain structures. Early signs of ICP are often subtle and include changes in mental status, such as irritability, restlessness, or altered behavior. As pressure continues to rise, late signs develop, including Cushing’s triad (bradycardia, widened pulse pressure, and irregular respirations), which indicate severe neurological compromise. Early recognition is critical because timely intervention can prevent brain herniation and permanent neurological damage.

(Correct Option 2). A change in behavior is an early sign of increased ICP. Children may become irritable, restless, confused, or less responsive. These subtle neurological changes are often the first indication of rising intracranial pressure and require prompt assessment and intervention.

(Option 1). Bradycardia is a late sign of increased ICP and is part of Cushing’s triad. It indicates significant brainstem compression and worsening neurological status.

(Option 3). Widened pulse pressure is also a late sign associated with Cushing’s triad. It reflects advanced increased ICP and impaired cerebral perfusion.

(Option 4). Changes in respiratory rate and pattern are late manifestations of increased ICP, often indicating brainstem involvement and severe neurological deterioration.

Educational Objective
Early signs of increased intracranial pressure include behavioral and mental status changes. Late signs, such as bradycardia, widened pulse pressure, and abnormal respirations, indicate severe and potentially life-threatening progression.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Increased Intracranial Pressure

answerChoiceList.choice: Bradycardia
Change in behavior
Widened pulse pressure
Change in respiratory rate and pattern

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for several children who have been fitted with a variety of traction devices. Which child should be monitored most closely for signs and symptoms of osteomyelitis?
questionList.explanationText:
Osteomyelitis is an infection of the bone that can occur when microorganisms enter through the bloodstream or directly through an opening in the skin and bone. In children receiving traction, the greatest risk occurs with skeletal traction because pins or wires are inserted directly into the bone, creating a potential entry site for infection. Manifestations may include fever, localized pain, redness, swelling, drainage at the pin site, and elevated inflammatory markers. In contrast, traction methods that use only skin attachment devices do not penetrate bone and therefore carry a much lower risk of osteomyelitis. Careful monitoring of pin sites and strict aseptic technique are essential to prevent serious bone infection.

(Correct Option 2). Image option B shows a child in skeletal traction, which involves pins, wires, or a fixation device attached directly to the bone. Because the bone is penetrated, this child has the highest risk for developing osteomyelitis. The nurse should closely monitor for pin-site redness, warmth, swelling, purulent drainage, fever, and increasing pain, all of which may signal infection extending into the bone.

(Option 1). Image option A represents a form of skin traction, in which the traction is applied externally to the skin and soft tissues rather than through the bone. Although skin irritation or impaired circulation can occur, the risk of osteomyelitis is much lower because there is no direct bony penetration.

(Option 3). Image option C shows cervical or head traction equipment. Unless pins are specifically inserted into the skull, this setup is not the classic pediatric skeletal traction device most associated with osteomyelitis risk in this comparison. It does not present the same direct long-bone pin-site infection risk as skeletal traction in the extremity.

(Option 4). Image option D also represents skin traction applied to the lower extremity. This device can cause skin breakdown, pressure injury, or neurovascular compromise, but it does not involve pins passing into bone, so the risk of osteomyelitis is less than with skeletal traction.

Educational Objective
Children in skeletal traction are at greatest risk for osteomyelitis because pins or wires are inserted into bone. Nurses should perform frequent pin-site assessments and monitor for fever, drainage, redness, and increasing pain.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Traction and Osteomyelitis Risk

questionList.questionText: A nurse is assigned to care for an infant with congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH). Which clinical finding supports this diagnosis?
questionList.explanationText:
Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) is a developmental defect in the diaphragm that allows abdominal viscera, such as the stomach or intestines, to move into the thoracic cavity during fetal development. This displacement compresses the developing lungs and leads to pulmonary hypoplasia and varying degrees of respiratory distress after birth. Because the herniated organs occupy space in the chest, the mediastinum and heart may shift away from their normal position. As a result, assessment findings may include diminished breath sounds on the affected side, a scaphoid abdomen, and displacement of cardiac sounds to the opposite side of the chest.

(Correct Option 4). Auscultation of cardiac sounds on the right side of the chest supports the diagnosis of congenital diaphragmatic hernia because herniation of abdominal contents into the left thoracic cavity can push the mediastinum and heart to the right. This is a classic finding in infants with a left-sided CDH, which is the most common type. The shift occurs because the bowel and other viscera occupy space in the chest and compress the normal thoracic structures, making right-sided cardiac auscultation an important clinical clue.

(Option 1). Presence of an anal membrane is incorrect because it is associated with imperforate anus, a congenital anorectal malformation, not with a diaphragmatic defect. This finding involves obstruction at the anal opening rather than displacement of abdominal organs into the chest.

(Option 2). Failure to pass meconium stool is incorrect because it is more suggestive of disorders such as Hirschsprung disease, meconium ileus, or other forms of intestinal obstruction. Although CDH involves abdominal organs, the hallmark findings are primarily related to respiratory compromise and thoracic organ displacement, not delayed meconium passage.

(Option 3). Viscera located outside the abdominal cavity is incorrect because this finding is characteristic of omphalocele or gastroschisis, in which abdominal organs protrude externally through the abdominal wall. In CDH, the viscera move internally into the thoracic cavity, not outside the body.

Educational Objective
Congenital diaphragmatic hernia occurs when abdominal contents herniate through a defect in the diaphragm into the chest, causing lung compression and mediastinal shift. Key findings include respiratory distress, decreased breath sounds, a scaphoid abdomen, and displaced cardiac sounds, often heard on the right side in a left-sided hernia.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia

answerChoiceList.choice: Presence of an anal membrane
Failure to pass meconium stool
Viscera located outside the abdominal cavity
Auscultation of cardiac sounds on the right side of the chest

questionList.questionText: A nurse is providing information to parents about the transmission of hepatitis. The nurse should tell the parents that hepatitis A virus (HAV) is primarily transmitted:
questionList.explanationText:
Hepatitis A virus (HAV) is an acute viral infection that primarily affects the liver and is transmitted by the fecal-oral route. Infection usually occurs when a person ingests food or water contaminated with infected stool, often because of poor hand hygiene, inadequate sanitation, or unsafe food handling. HAV does not usually cause chronic liver disease, but it can produce symptoms such as jaundice, fatigue, nausea, abdominal discomfort, and elevated liver enzymes. Because children may have mild or no symptoms, they can still spread the infection through contaminated food, water, or close household contact, making prevention through hygiene and vaccination especially important.

(Correct Option 4). Hepatitis A virus is primarily transmitted in contaminated food or water, which reflects the classic fecal-oral mode of transmission. This means the virus enters the body when a person consumes something contaminated by stool from an infected individual. Teaching parents this route of spread is essential because prevention depends heavily on proper handwashing, safe food preparation, clean water, and immunization when indicated.

(Option 1). During birth is incorrect because this type of transmission is more closely associated with hepatitis B and sometimes other bloodborne viral infections. Vertical transmission from mother to infant is not the primary route for hepatitis A.

(Option 2). By way of sexual contact is incorrect because although HAV can rarely spread through certain close personal or sexual practices involving fecal exposure, this is not the primary transmission route emphasized in basic parent teaching. Sexual transmission is more classically associated with hepatitis B infection.

(Option 3). In blood and blood products is incorrect because bloodborne transmission is most characteristic of hepatitis B and hepatitis C. HAV is mainly spread through ingestion of contaminated substances rather than exposure to infected blood products.

Educational Objective
Hepatitis A is spread mainly by the fecal-oral route, especially through contaminated food or water. Parent teaching should focus on hand hygiene, sanitation, safe food handling, and vaccination to reduce transmission risk.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Hepatitis A Transmission

answerChoiceList.choice: During birth
By way of sexual contact
In blood and blood products
In contaminated food or water

questionList.questionText: A pancreatic enzyme preparation is prescribed for a child with cystic fibrosis (CF). The nurse instructs the child’s mother to administer the pancreatic enzyme:
questionList.explanationText:
Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetic disorder that affects the exocrine glands, leading to thick, sticky secretions that obstruct the pancreatic ducts. This obstruction prevents the release of essential digestive enzymes such as lipase, amylase, and protease into the small intestine. As a result, children with CF experience malabsorption, especially of fats and fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), leading to steatorrhea, poor weight gain, and nutritional deficiencies. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy is necessary to aid in proper digestion and nutrient absorption, and it must be timed appropriately with food intake to be effective.

(Correct Option 2). Pancreatic enzymes should be administered with meals and snacks because they need to be present in the gastrointestinal tract at the same time as food to assist in digestion. This ensures optimal breakdown and absorption of nutrients, particularly fats. Giving enzymes at the beginning or during meals allows them to mix with ingested food, improving nutritional status and preventing complications such as malnutrition and poor growth in children with CF.

(Option 1). At noon only is incorrect because enzyme therapy must be administered consistently with every food intake, not just once a day. Limiting administration to noon would result in inadequate digestion of other meals and snacks, leading to poor nutrient absorption.

(Option 3). 2 hours after breakfast and dinner is incorrect because enzymes must be present during digestion, not after the digestive process has already begun. Delayed administration reduces their effectiveness and does not adequately support nutrient breakdown.

(Option 4). At bedtime and in the morning when the child awakens is incorrect because enzyme supplementation is only needed when food is consumed. Administering enzymes without food provides no benefit and does not aid in digestion.

Educational Objective
Pancreatic enzyme replacement in cystic fibrosis should always be given with meals and snacks to ensure proper digestion and absorption of nutrients, especially fats, and to prevent malnutrition.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Cystic Fibrosis Management

answerChoiceList.choice: At noon only
With meals and snacks
2 hours after breakfast and dinner
At bedtime and in the morning when the child awakens

standards.description: Physiological Integrity - Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questionList.questionText: A nurse is monitoring a 3-year-old with diarrhea for signs of dehydration. The child now weighs 42 lb, a decrease from his weight of 44 lb 24 hours ago. In addition to dry mucous membranes and lack of tears, what assessment finding would the nurse find?
questionList.explanationText:
Dehydration in children occurs when there is excessive loss of body fluids, commonly due to diarrhea, vomiting, or inadequate intake. In pediatric patients, even small fluid losses can quickly lead to hypovolemia because of their higher metabolic rate and lower fluid reserves. Clinical manifestations include weight loss, dry mucous membranes, absence of tears, decreased skin turgor, and changes in circulatory status. As dehydration progresses, there is reduced circulating volume, resulting in weak peripheral pulses, delayed capillary refill, tachycardia, and decreased urine output. Weight loss of 2 lb in 24 hours is significant and indicates moderate dehydration in a young child.

(Correct Option 2). Bilateral 1+ pedal pulses indicate weak peripheral perfusion, which is consistent with dehydration and reduced circulating blood volume. As fluid loss occurs, the body prioritizes blood flow to vital organs, leading to diminished pulses in the extremities. This finding aligns with other signs such as dry mucous membranes and lack of tears, confirming ongoing dehydration.

(Option 1). Decreased heart rate is incorrect because dehydration typically causes tachycardia as a compensatory response to maintain cardiac output. A decreased heart rate would not be expected and may indicate a different or more severe clinical issue.

(Option 3). Increased blood pressure is incorrect because dehydration usually leads to decreased blood pressure due to reduced intravascular volume. Hypotension may occur in more advanced stages of dehydration.

(Option 4). Urine output of 80 mL in the last 3 hours is incorrect because this reflects relatively adequate urine output for a child, suggesting sufficient hydration. In dehydration, urine output typically decreases, and urine becomes more concentrated.

Educational Objective
In pediatric dehydration, key findings include weight loss, dry mucous membranes, absence of tears, tachycardia, decreased urine output, and weak peripheral pulses. Early recognition of these signs is essential for prompt fluid replacement and prevention of complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Dehydration in Children

answerChoiceList.choice: Decreased heart rate
Bilateral 1+ pedal pulses
Increase blood pressure
Urine output of 80 mL in the last 3 hours

standards.description: Physiological Integrity - Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText:
A child is admitted to the hospital with suspected infective endocarditis. List in order of priority how the nurse will carry out the physician’s prescriptions for the child, with 1 being the first prescription to be carried out and 4 the last.

1. Scheduling an echocardiogram
2. Administering intravenous (IV) antibiotics
3. Starting an IV line
4. Obtaining blood for cultures
questionList.explanationText:
Infective endocarditis is a serious infection of the endocardial surface of the heart, usually involving the heart valves. It occurs when microorganisms enter the bloodstream and adhere to damaged endocardium, forming infected vegetations. Because the diagnosis depends heavily on identifying the causative organism, it is essential to obtain blood cultures before starting antimicrobial therapy whenever possible. After cultures are obtained, the nurse should ensure vascular access is established so that prescribed intravenous antibiotics can be started promptly. Diagnostic studies such as an echocardiogram help confirm the presence of vegetations and assess cardiac function, but they are performed after urgent culture collection and treatment preparation are underway.

(Correct Option 4). The correct priority sequence is 4, 3, 2, 1. The nurse should first obtain blood for cultures so the organism causing infective endocarditis can be identified before antibiotics alter the results. Next, the nurse should start an IV line to establish reliable access for medication administration. Once access is in place, the nurse should administer IV antibiotics promptly because early treatment is critical to reduce complications such as valve destruction, embolization, and sepsis. Scheduling an echocardiogram is important for diagnosis and evaluation, but it follows the immediate priorities of culture collection, access, and treatment initiation.

(Option 1). The sequence 1, 4, 3, 2 is incorrect because scheduling an echocardiogram is not the first priority when infective endocarditis is suspected. Blood cultures must be obtained first, and treatment preparation should not be delayed for a diagnostic study.

(Option 2). The sequence 3, 2, 1, 4 is incorrect because giving antibiotics before obtaining blood cultures can reduce the likelihood of isolating the causative organism. This can interfere with accurate diagnosis and targeted antimicrobial therapy.

(Option 3). The sequence 3, 4, 1, 2 is incorrect because although starting an IV line is necessary, blood cultures should be prioritized first when feasible and before antibiotics are started. In addition, delaying IV antibiotics until after scheduling the echocardiogram does not reflect appropriate urgency.

Educational Objective
In suspected infective endocarditis, obtain blood cultures before antibiotics to identify the organism accurately. Then establish IV access, begin prescribed IV antibiotics promptly, and complete diagnostic studies such as echocardiography after immediate treatment priorities are addressed.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Infective Endocarditis Priorities

questionList.questionText: A nurse is preparing to administer digoxin (Lanoxin) to an infant. The nurse notes that the infant’s heart rate is 110 beats/min. The appropriate response on the part of the nurse is to:
questionList.explanationText:
Digoxin (Lanoxin) is a cardiac glycoside used to improve cardiac contractility and regulate heart rate in conditions such as heart failure and certain arrhythmias. It works by increasing intracellular calcium, leading to stronger myocardial contractions, and by slowing conduction through the atrioventricular (AV) node. Because digoxin has a narrow therapeutic index, careful monitoring of the apical heart rate is essential before administration. In infants, digoxin should generally be withheld if the heart rate is less than 90–100 beats/min, depending on institutional guidelines. A heart rate of 110 beats/min in an infant falls within the normal range, indicating that it is safe to administer the medication as prescribed.

(Correct Option 2). The nurse should administer the prescribed dose because a heart rate of 110 beats/min is within the acceptable range for an infant and does not indicate bradycardia. Since digoxin should only be withheld when the heart rate falls below the recommended threshold, there is no contraindication to giving the medication. Administering the dose ensures continued therapeutic benefit in managing the infant’s cardiac condition.

(Option 1). Contact the healthcare provider is incorrect because there is no abnormal finding requiring clarification. The infant’s heart rate is within normal limits, so there is no need to delay treatment or seek additional orders.

(Option 3). Obtain a blood sample to check the digoxin level is incorrect because routine serum level monitoring is not required prior to every dose unless toxicity is suspected. There are no signs of digoxin toxicity in this scenario.

(Option 4). Withhold the dose and reassess the heart rate in 1 hour is incorrect because withholding digoxin is only appropriate when the heart rate is below the safe threshold. A heart rate of 110 beats/min does not warrant delaying the medication.

Educational Objective
Before administering digoxin, always assess the apical heart rate. In infants, withhold the medication if the heart rate is below 90–100 beats/min. A normal heart rate indicates that it is safe to administer the prescribed dose.
questionList.subject: Pharmacology
questionList.title: Digoxin Administration

answerChoiceList.choice: Contact the healthcare provider
Administer the prescribed dose
Obtain a blood sample to check the digoxin level
Withhold the dose and reassess the heart rate in 1 hour
